text: Srikanth Nammalwar Kidambi (born 7 February 1993) is an Indian badminton player. In 2014, he won the China Open Super Series Premier, the first Indian to do so. In 2022, he was part of the Indian team that won the Thomas Cup. As of September 2022, he is ranked 11th in the world.
*Beatrijs* was a Dutch Catholic women's magazine founded on January 5, 1939. Known for its modest content, it ceased publication in 1967 after declining circulation.
1. FC Kaan-Marienborn is a German association football club based in Netphen, North Rhine-Westphalia, founded in 2007.

Greg Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician and a Republican member of the Arkansas State Senate, representing the 16th district from 2015 until his death. He won a special election to succeed Michael Lamoureux. Standridge died of cancer at the age of 50.

The architecture of the Deccan sultanates reflects the distinctive blend of Persian, Indian, and regional styles developed under the Bahmani and its successor sultanates—Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Bijapur, Golkonda, and Gulbarga. Notable structures include Ahmadnagar's palaces (Farah Bakhsh Bagh, Hasht Bihisht Bagh) and tombs; Bidar's grand fort, Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, and Bahmani tombs; and Bijapur's monumental Gol Gumbaz (with one of the world's largest pre-modern domes) and Ibrahim Rouza. Golkonda is renowned for its impregnable fort, Qutb Shahi tombs, and iconic Charminar, while Gulbarga features early Bahmani monuments like the Great Mosque and Haft Gumbad complex. These sultanates left a lasting architectural legacy, characterized by massive domes, intricate stucco work, and innovative urban planning.

Barry Ono (born Frederick Valentine Harrison; 21 May 1876 – ?) was a British comedian, songwriter, and performer known for his one-man routine *The Old Time Music Hall*. He was also an avid collector of penny fiction.

Michael Kevin O'Loughlin (born 20 February 1977) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Sydney Swans in the Australian Football League (AFL). Nicknamed "Micky O" and "Magic Mick", he was an All-Australian twice (1997, 2000), won Sydney's best and fairest award in 1998, and played in the Swans' 2005 premiership team. O'Loughlin was also the club's leading goalkicker twice (2000–01). In 2009, he was awarded the Madden Medal. After retiring, he coached the Indigenous All-Stars and co-founded the GO Foundation with Adam Goodes, focusing on education for Indigenous Australians.

Charles Hargens (1893–1997) was an American illustrator and painter known for his detailed Western-themed artwork. Born in South Dakota, he studied at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts and later in Paris. His career included magazine illustrations for *The Saturday Evening Post* and *Collier's*, as well as advertisements for brands like Coca-Cola. A meticulous researcher, he emphasized historical accuracy in his depictions of the American West. Many of his works, including his Carversville studio collection, were donated to the Dakota Discovery Museum in South Dakota.

O RLY? is an Internet meme featuring a snowy owl accompanied by the phrase "O RLY?" (an abbreviation of "Oh, really?"). The meme has been referenced in popular culture, including video games like *World of Warcraft*, and inspired parodies such as altered O’Reilly Media book covers. In 2006, it was associated with the "W32/Hoots-A" computer worm, which displayed the meme's image on infected systems. The meme remains a notable example of early Internet humor.

*The Stone Flower* is a 1946 Soviet fantasy film directed by Aleksandr Ptushko, based on Pavel Bazhov's fairy tale of the same name. It stars Vladimir Druzhnikov as Danilo, a young and talented gem cutter who seeks to capture the true beauty of stone. His quest leads him to the Mistress of the Copper Mountain and the legendary Stone Flower, a sight that is said to enchant those who behold it. The film follows Danilo's journey, his relationship with his fiancée Katinka, and his ultimate choice between the allure of the Stone Flower and his love for Katinka.

Yann Weymouth is an American architect known for his work on the National Gallery of Art East Wing and the Grand Louvre Pyramid. He has received numerous accolades, including being named a "Chevalier" of the Ordre National du Mérite by French President François Mitterrand.
Stephen Tallents (born in London) was a British civil servant, military officer, and public relations pioneer, known for his leadership at the Empire Marketing Board and contributions to the BBC.

The Bernina railway line is a high-altitude railway in the Swiss canton of Graubünden, connecting St. Moritz, Switzerland, to Tirano, Italy, via the Bernina Pass. Opened in stages between 1908 and 1910, it was originally built by the Bernina-Bahngesellschaft to provide access to hydroelectric plants and promote tourism in the region. The line, which operates year-round, is notable for its steep gradients, reaching a maximum of 7% without the use of rack-and-pinion technology, making it one of the steepest adhesion railways in the world. It also holds the distinction of being the highest railway in the Alps with year-round service, peaking at 2,253 meters (7,392 feet) above sea level.

Now operated by the Rhaetian Railway, the Bernina line is part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Rhaetian Railway in the Albula / Bernina Landscapes." It is renowned for its engineering marvels, including the Brusio spiral viaduct and the Montebello Curve, as well as its scenic views of glaciers, lakes, and alpine landscapes. The line serves both passenger and freight traffic, with flagship services like the Bernina Express attracting tourists worldwide. It remains a vital link for local communities and a celebrated example of mountain railway engineering.

Oxhorn Run is a 1.8-mile-long stream located in Davidson Township, Sullivan County, Pennsylvania, with its source at an elevation between 2,340 and 2,360 feet. It flows southeast, deepening into a valley before joining Painter Run. The stream's watershed covers 0.87 square miles and is part of the Upper Susquehanna-Lackawanna drainage basin. Oxhorn Run is classified as impaired due to atmospheric deposition and pH issues, failing to meet Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection standards.

Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter. He played college football for the Florida Gators, winning a BCS National Championship in 2009 and the Ray Guy Award in 2010. Henry was signed by the Philadelphia Eagles as an undrafted free agent in 2011.

"The Moral Obligation to Be Intelligent" is a 1915 philosophical essay by American scholar John Erskine. It argues that intelligence is not merely a personal advantage but a moral duty. The essay gained renewed attention when included in *Gateway to the Great Books* (1963) and later in a posthumous collection of Erskine's works (2000), edited by his former student Lionel Trilling. Trilling, who also taught Columbia University's "Great Books" course, selected the essay as the title piece for the volume, emphasizing its central theme. The work remains influential in discussions on ethics and intellectual responsibility.

The French brig *Sphinx* was a *Sylphe*-class warship built in Genoa in 1813 for the French Navy. Captured by the British in April 1814, she was renamed *Sphynx* and later *Regent*, serving as a revenue cruiser. Sold in 1824, she was possibly acquired by Colombia and renamed *Victoria*, disappearing from records by 1828. The brig was armed with carronades and chase guns, with a displacement of around 374 tons.

Douglas Keszler is an American chemist and distinguished professor at Oregon State University, known for his research in inorganic and materials chemistry. His work includes developing hydrothermal dehydration techniques for solid synthesis and advancing amorphous oxide semiconductors for electronic displays. He earned his Ph.D. from Northwestern University in 1984 and has since contributed to innovations in thin-film transistors and aqueous-based nanomaterial synthesis.

Patillas, Puerto Rico is a coastal municipality in southeastern Puerto Rico, known as *"La Esmeralda del Sur"* (The Emerald of the South) for its lush landscapes. Founded in 1811, it features mountains, beaches, and the Patillas Reservoir. The town's economy historically relied on sugarcane and now includes plantain production. With a 2020 population of 15,985, it is governed by Mayor Maritza Sánchez Neris. Patillas celebrates its patron saint festival in August and is home to 28 beaches.

The "mercy rule" is a regulation in sports designed to end or modify a game when one team has an insurmountable lead, preventing further humiliation for the losing team and reducing unnecessary playtime. It is commonly applied in youth, high school, and amateur sports, with variations across sports and governing bodies. In American football, many states implement a "continuous clock" once a team leads by a certain margin, while in baseball and softball, games may end early if a team leads by a specified number of runs after a set number of innings. Similarly, high school basketball often uses a running clock when a lead reaches a certain threshold.

In college football, the NCAA allows coaches to agree to shorten game periods, though a running clock is not permitted. Other sports, such as soccer, boxing, and wrestling, have their own mercy rules, including ending games or matches when a specific point differential is reached. International sports like cricket and curling have no formal mercy rules but include mechanisms to expedite play. The mercy rule aims to balance sportsmanship with competitive integrity, ensuring games remain fair and enjoyable for all participants.

W. & J. Sloane was a prominent American retail company specializing in rugs, furniture, and home furnishings, founded in 1843 by William Sloane in New York City. Renowned for introducing oriental carpets to the U.S. and setting national decorating trends, it served elite clients, including the White House. After decades of success, the company declined following its 1955 acquisition, filing for bankruptcy in 1985.

The Sportpalast speech, delivered by Joseph Goebbels on February 18, 1943, in Berlin, was a pivotal moment in Nazi Germany's propaganda during World War II. Addressing a carefully selected and fervent audience at the Berlin Sportpalast, Goebbels called for "total war" to counter the growing Allied advances following the Axis defeats at Stalingrad, North Africa, and in the Pacific. The speech marked the Nazi leadership's first public acknowledgment of Germany's dire military situation and launched a campaign for full national mobilization, including austerity measures and the closure of non-essential establishments.

Goebbels framed the war as a struggle against "International Jewry" and Bolshevism, using inflammatory rhetoric to justify radical policies. The speech is historically significant for its role in prolonging the war and for its propagandistic fervor, as Goebbels sought to rally the German population and gain greater control over the war economy. While millions listened via radio, the speech's recorded and written versions reveal differences, including Goebbels' near-mention of the "extermination" of Jews. Despite its immediate impact, the speech also inspired late-war jokes reflecting growing disillusionment among Germans.

Jefferson Andrés Lerma Solís (born 25 October 1994) is a Colombian professional footballer who plays as a defensive midfielder for Premier League club AFC Bournemouth and the Colombia national team. Lerma began his career with Atlético Huila in Colombia, where he made his professional debut in 2013 and became a key player. In 2015, he joined Spanish club Levante, initially on loan, and helped them achieve promotion to La Liga during the 2016–17 season. 

In 2018, Lerma signed with Bournemouth for a club-record fee, becoming a fan favorite for his tough-tackling style. He played a pivotal role in Bournemouth's promotion back to the Premier League in the 2021–22 season. Internationally, Lerma has represented Colombia since 2017, featuring in the 2018 FIFA World Cup and the 2019 Copa América. Known for his versatility and physicality, he has also scored key goals for both club and country.

*Soul Power* is the debut studio album by American musician Curtis Harding, released on May 6, 2014, by Burger Records. Recorded at Living Room Studios in Atlanta, the album blends soul with influences from gospel, indie rock, garage, and disco. Harding's versatile vocals and genre-spanning compositions received critical praise, though some critics noted a lack of innovation.

The Alliance française is an international organization that aims to promote French language and culture around the world. It was founded in Paris on July 21, 1883, by figures such as Louis Pasteur, Ferdinand de Lesseps, and Jules Verne. The organization is financed by course fees, rental income, and a subsidy from the French government.

The Alliance comprises a network of independently run local chapters. The Alliance française Paris Ile-de-France, located in Paris, is a private higher education institute and the oldest school in the network.

Otto Eisenschiml (June 16, 1880 – December 7, 1963) was an Austrian-American chemist and historian. A successful industrial chemist, he invented a one-piece window envelope and developed tests for oil contaminants. He is best known for his controversial 1937 book *Why Was Lincoln Murdered?*, which proposed that Secretary of War Edwin M. Stanton orchestrated Abraham Lincoln's assassination. Though widely dismissed by historians, his theory influenced popular culture.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (1888–1964) was a prominent Russian chemist and biochemist known for his research on essential oils, terpenes, and vegetable fats. He held various academic positions at Leningrad State University and contributed significantly to the field of organic chemistry, publishing over 200 works and receiving several awards, including the Order of Lenin.
**Stockland Bristol** is a village and civil parish in the Sedgemoor district of Somerset, England, with a population of 165 in 2011.

The 22nd Seanad Éireann first met on September 12, 2002. It comprised 43 senators elected from vocational panels, 6 from universities, and 11 nominated by the Taoiseach. During its term, one senator was replaced after death, and four Taoiseach nominees were replaced after being elected to the Dáil. Ten elected senators also won seats in the Dáil, but their seats remained vacant.

Cape Newenham LRRS Airport (IATA: EHM, ICAO: PAEH) is a military airstrip located in Cape Newenham, Alaska, owned by the U.S. Air Force. Established in 1951, it serves the Cape Newenham Long Range Radar Station, providing access for maintenance personnel and supplies. The airport features a gravel runway and operates year-round, though it may be inaccessible during severe winter weather.

Ariyalur is a town and the administrative headquarters of Ariyalur district in the state of Tamil Nadu, India. Located in the Chola Nadu region, it is historically significant as part of the Chola dynasty, with Gangai Konda Cholapuram serving as the capital during the late Chola period. The town later came under the rule of the Vijayanagar Nayaks, Marathas, and eventually the British. Ariyalur is known for its rich limestone resources, which support a thriving cement industry, with major companies like UltraTech Cement and Dalmia Cement operating in the area. Agriculture, particularly sugarcane, cashew, and rice cultivation, also plays a significant role in the local economy.

Ariyalur has a hot semi-arid climate and is connected by National Highway NH136 and a railway station on the Chennai–Tiruchirappalli line. The municipality, established in 1943, oversees local governance and infrastructure, including water supply, waste management, and healthcare. As of the 2011 census, the town had a population of 28,902, with a literacy rate of 76.04%. Ariyalur is part of the Chidambaram Lok Sabha constituency and the Ariyalur State Assembly constituency. The town is also home to historical landmarks, including temples built during the Vijayanagar period, and serves as a hub for transportation and commerce in the region.

The Ligier JS29 is a Formula One racing car designed by Ligier for the 1987 season. Initially intended to be powered by an Alfa Romeo engine, the JS29 faced a significant setback when Alfa Romeo withdrew from the project, leading Ligier to adopt the Megatron straight-four turbo engine instead. The car made its competitive debut at the 1987 San Marino Grand Prix, with drivers René Arnoux and Piercarlo Ghinzani. Despite its challenges, including engine supply issues and performance limitations, the JS29B achieved a best finish of sixth place, marking Ligier's only point of the season. The JS29 was ultimately replaced by the JS31 in 1988.

The Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad was an interurban railway in Sonoma County, California, formed in 1903 through the consolidation of four horse car lines. Electrification enabled service between Petaluma and Santa Rosa, with extensions to Sebastopol and Forestville. A notable event in its history was the "Battle of Sebastopol Road" in 1905, a conflict with a steam railroad over a crossing. Expansion plans were disrupted by the 1906 San Francisco earthquake. Passenger service declined, ceasing entirely in 1932 after being purchased by Northwestern Pacific Railroad. The line was gradually dismantled between 1931 and 1989.

Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.873-mile-long (17.498 km) state highway located in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania. Its southern terminus is at U.S. Route 322 (US 322) and PA 241 in South Annville Township, and its northern terminus is at Interstate 81 (I-81) in Fort Indiantown Gap.

The route heads north-northwest from US 322/PA 241, passing through agricultural areas and the community of Annville, where it intersects US 422. North of Annville, PA 934 runs near Lebanon Valley College before heading into areas of farms, woods, and homes. The route continues through Bellegrove and Harper Tavern, where it has an interchange with US 22. From there, PA 934 widens to a divided highway before reaching its northern terminus at I-81, where the road continues north as an unsigned quadrant route onto Fort Indiantown Gap.

PA 934 was first designated in 1928, running from Annville to Bellegrove. Over the years, it was extended south to US 322/PA 241 and north to US 22 north of Harper Tavern. In the 1960s, it was extended further north to PA 72 in Lickdale, replacing a section of PA 343. The route was realigned in the late 1960s before being cut back to its current northern terminus at I-81 in the 1970s.
Maria Clara Eimmart (1676–1707) was a German astronomer and engraver. She is best known for her astronomical illustrations, particularly of the phases of the Moon.

Jody Hamilton (born Joseph Nicholas Zwaduk III; August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer, best known for his work as "The Assassin" and later "The Flame." Debuting in 1955, Hamilton achieved fame as part of the masked tag team "The Assassins" alongside Tom Renesto, becoming one of the most notorious and successful tag teams in wrestling history. The duo held multiple championships, including a record 12 NWA Georgia Tag Team Championships, and headlined events worldwide. Hamilton also found success as a singles competitor, holding titles such as the NWA Central States Heavyweight Championship.

After retiring from in-ring competition in the 1980s due to a back injury, Hamilton became a prominent trainer, running World Championship Wrestling's Power Plant, where he trained stars like Bill Goldberg and Kevin Nash. He later partnered with WWE to establish Deep South Wrestling as a developmental territory. Hamilton also worked with Rampage Pro Wrestling and was involved in a legal dispute with WWE, which was settled in 2010. 

Hamilton's son, Nick Patrick, followed in his footsteps as a wrestling referee. Hamilton passed away on August 3, 2021, at the age of 82, leaving behind a legacy as a wrestler, trainer, and influential figure in professional wrestling.

DWOW (103.5 FM), branded as K-Lite 103.5, is an adult contemporary radio station in Metro Manila, Philippines, owned by Advanced Media Broadcasting System. Launched in 1979 as DWIM-FM, it has undergone multiple rebrandings, including DWCS, Heart FM, Max FM, and Wow FM, before reverting to K-Lite in 2013. The station plays hits from the 1990s to the 2000s and is also simulcast on ALLTV.

A quick coupler is a device used on construction and earth-moving machines to allow for the rapid exchange of work tools or buckets. They are most common on hydraulic excavators, compact excavators, and backhoe loaders. There are manual, semi-automatic, and automatic types, each with different designs and safety considerations.

Abdulai Osman Conteh (born August 5, 1945) is a Sierra Leonean lawyer, politician, and jurist who served as the 9th Chief Justice of Belize from 2000 to 2010. He previously held prominent political roles in Sierra Leone, including Minister of Foreign Affairs, Minister of Finance, Attorney-General, and First Vice-President. Conteh is known for his landmark rulings on constitutional and indigenous land rights in Belize and his judicial service in the Caribbean.

Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya. Educated at Trinity College, Kandy, and the University of Peradeniya, he later earned a Doctor of Medicine from the University of Birmingham and trained at the Mayo Clinic. He played a key role in introducing liver transplantation to Sri Lanka. A fellow of multiple surgical colleges, Deen has held visiting professorships in India and Malaysia and has published extensively in medical research.

Colt Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American football tight end who played college football at the University of Oregon. He gained recognition for his performance in high school, winning a state championship and earning all-state honors. Lyerla was signed as an undrafted free agent by the Green Bay Packers in 2014 but faced injuries that limited his NFL career. He later played in the Arena Football League for the Arizona Rattlers and Portland Steel. Lyerla's career has been marred by legal issues, including drug-related arrests and a prison sentence.
Patrick Pothuizen (born 1972) is a Dutch football coach and former player. He holds the Eredivisie record for most yellow cards received. He currently manages Achilles '29.

The Yawalapiti language is a critically endangered Arawakan language spoken by the Yawalapiti people in Brazil's Xingu Indigenous Park. With only eight native speakers reported in 2006, it is closely related to Waurá and features a phonemic inventory with oral and nasal vowels, glottal stops, and complex morphophonemic processes. The language uses agglutinative suffixes for possession and classifiers, follows an SVO word order, and distinguishes alienable and inalienable nouns.

Marcus Bontempelli (born 24 November 1995) is an Australian rules footballer and captain of the Western Bulldogs in the Australian Football League (AFL). Drafted fourth overall in the 2013 AFL draft, he made his debut in 2014 and quickly established himself as a key player, winning the AFLPA Best First-Year Player award. Bontempelli played a pivotal role in the Bulldogs' 2016 premiership victory and has since earned multiple accolades, including four All-Australian selections (2016, 2019–2021), four club best-and-fairest awards (Charles Sutton Medal), and the 2021 Leigh Matthews Trophy as the AFL's most valuable player. Known for his leadership, he was named Bulldogs captain in 2020. Off the field, he is an advocate for social causes, including marriage equality and youth mentoring.

Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness (died November 28, 1746), was a Scottish landowner known for his involvement in the Jacobite uprising of 1745, supporting Charles Edward Stuart against the Hanoverian Crown. The son of Alexander Wedderburn, the 4th Baronet, he faced financial difficulties and raised his family in modest conditions. Captured at the Battle of Culloden, Wedderburn was tried for treason and executed in London. He married Jean Fullerton in 1724 and had several children, including two sons, John and James, and daughters who were later cared for by their relative, portrait painter Catherine Read.

Benton Township is a township in Adair County, Missouri, covering 80.26 square miles (207.9 km²) with a population of 19,700 as of 2010. It includes the county seat, Kirksville, and is home to several cemeteries, streams, and educational institutions, including A.T. Still University and Truman State University. Major highways and two heliports serve the area.

Vibal Publishing House, founded in 1953 by Hilarion P. Vibal and Esther Asuncion-Vibal, is a leading Philippine publishing company headquartered in Quezon City. Specializing in educational materials, it produces textbooks, e-books, and other learning resources. Over the decades, Vibal has expanded into digital education, introducing multimedia materials, digital classrooms, and low-cost tablets to support Philippine schools. It has received multiple National Book Development Board Quality Seal Awards and continues to innovate in education through partnerships with organizations like Microsoft and initiatives like the Center for Digital Education.

Leif Carlsen (4 December 1943 – 14 September 2018) was a Danish footballer who played as a forward for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Landskrona BoIS, helping the latter secure promotion to Sweden's top division.

The foramen rotundum is a circular aperture in the sphenoid bone of the skull, allowing the passage of the maxillary nerve, an artery, and an emissary vein. It undergoes shape changes during development, achieving a round form in adulthood.

Tamika Tremaglio is an American business executive known for her leadership roles at Deloitte and the National Basketball Players Association (NBPA). Born in St. Mary's County, Maryland, she holds a Bachelor of Arts in Business & Finance, a JD, and an MBA. Tremaglio has received several honors, including Managing Partner of the Year by the Washington Business Journal and recognition as one of Washington's Most Powerful Women.

"We Say Mabuhay" is a swing/march song composed by Tirso Cruz with lyrics by James King Steele, first performed on May 16, 1931. It serves as the honors music for the President of the Philippines and is traditionally played at presidential inaugurations.

"Trapping the queen" refers to a tactical strategy in chess where a player's queen is forced into a position where it cannot escape capture. Notable examples include positions from games such as Alekhine-Rubinstein (1930) and Nezhmetdinov-Konstantinov (1936), illustrating various methods of achieving this tactic.

Riemann's differential equation is a second-order linear differential equation characterized by three regular singular points, typically denoted as \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\). The equation is associated with exponents \((\alpha, \alpha')\), \((\beta, \beta')\), and \((\gamma, \gamma')\) at these singular points, subject to specific constraints. Its solutions are commonly expressed using the Riemann P-symbol, which provides a compact representation of the equation's structure and its exponents.

The solutions of Riemann's differential equation are closely related to the hypergeometric function, with the standard hypergeometric function being expressible in terms of the P-symbol. Additionally, the equation's solutions exhibit a rich set of identities, including a connection to Kummer's 24 solutions of the hypergeometric differential equation.

Riemann's differential equation also possesses symmetry under fractional linear transformations, or Möbius transformations, which map the Riemann sphere conformally. These transformations preserve the structure of the equation while altering the singular points or exponents, highlighting its deep connections to complex analysis and geometry.

Bristol, Tennessee, is a city located in Sullivan County, known as "The Birthplace of Country Music." Incorporated in 1856, it was named after Bristol, England, and has a population of 27,147 as of the 2020 census. The city is recognized for its significant contributions to early country music, particularly through the Bristol Sessions in 1927, which featured influential artists like the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers. This musical heritage is celebrated at the Birthplace of Country Music Museum, which opened in 2014. Bristol hosts the annual Bristol Rhythm & Roots Reunion music festival, further solidifying its cultural legacy. Geographically, the city covers an area of 32.83 square miles and is situated in a humid subtropical climate zone. Bristol is also home to notable venues such as the Bristol Motor Speedway and Bristol Dragway, which host major motorsport events. The city is governed by a mayor and city manager, and its public education system is managed by Bristol Tennessee City Schools.

Egon Albrecht-Lemke (1918–1944) was a Luftwaffe Hauptmann (captain) during World War II. Credited with 25 aerial victories, he received the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross in May 1943. He was killed in action in August 1944, after being shot down over France.
Victor de Laprade (1812–1883) was a French poet and critic. He was elected to the National Assembly in 1871 and is remembered with a statue in Montbrison.
Scott Campbell (born December 28, 1973) is an American production designer, concept artist, and art director known for his work in video games, comics, and fine art.

Sanjog is a 1972 Indian Hindi film directed by S. S. Balan, featuring Amitabh Bachchan and Mala Sinha. The plot follows Mohan and Asha, whose marriage faces familial opposition, leading to separation. Years later, they reunite under complicated circumstances, culminating in tragedy.

*Fear* is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991, by Columbia Records. Recorded in January 1991 at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada, the album was produced by Gavin MacKillop, who also worked on the band's subsequent releases. *Fear* features a blend of alternative and pop rock and includes notable singles such as "All I Want," "Walk on the Ocean," and "Hold Her Down." Two tracks from the recording sessions, "Good Intentions" and "All in All," were excluded from the album but later appeared on the 1995 rarities collection *In Light Syrup*.
Hasan Zati Sungur (1898–1984) was a renowned Turkish stage magician known for his innovative performances and contributions to magic.

Woodall services is a motorway service area (MSA) on the M1 in South Yorkshire, England, operated by Welcome Break since 1968. Located near the Derbyshire border, it features fast-food outlets, a footbridge connecting both sides, and murals by David Fisher. Popular with coach travelers, it employs over 230 staff.

The Battle of Linlithgow Bridge took place in 1526 as a result of the power vacuum created by the death of James IV of Scotland. Margaret Tudor, mother of the young King James V, sought to free her son from the custody of Archibald Douglas, Earl of Angus. The Earl of Lennox raised an army to march on Edinburgh, but was met by James Hamilton, Earl of Arran, at Linlithgow Bridge. Lennox's forces were ultimately defeated by Arran and reinforcements led by Douglas and the King. The Earl of Lennox was killed after surrendering. The battle is a designated battlefield.

The *Cambridge Literary Review* (CLR) is a literary journal that publishes poetry, fiction, essays, and translations, with contributions from prominent and emerging writers. Founded in Cambridge, the journal has featured works by poets such as J.H. Prynne, Rae Armantrout, and Susan Howe, as well as essays by figures like Raymond Geuss and J.H. Prynne. Each issue often explores specific themes, such as translation or children's literature, and includes reprints of rare or historical texts. The journal has been edited by various contributors, including Lydia Wilson and Rosie Šnajdr, and has received critical attention, including reviews in the *Times Literary Supplement*.

Rhodium(III) chloride is the inorganic compound with the formula RhCl3. It is a dark red solid that is insoluble in water. RhCl3(H2O)3 is used as a precursor to prepare a variety of complexes, some of which are used in homogeneous catalysis. Beginning in the 1960s, it was found to catalyze reactions involving carbon monoxide, hydrogen, and alkenes.

Abubakar Umar Tutare (born February 13, 1965) is a Nigerian politician who served as the Senator for Taraba Central from June 2011 to June 2015, following a contentious election and prior roles in the Taraba State government.

Lieutenant General Sir Daril Watson, GCB, CBE, MC (1888–1967) was a British Army officer who served in both World War I and World War II. He later became Quartermaster-General to the Forces before retiring in 1947.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG) is a scholarly organization dedicated to the fields of anthropology and geography. Established in 1878, the society is known for its prestigious awards, including the Vega Medal, created in 1881 to honor Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld's discovery of the Northeast Passage. The medal is awarded approximately every three years to an outstanding physical geographer. The society also awards the SSAG Medal, previously named the Anders Retzius Medal, which was renamed in 2015 due to Retzius's controversial racial studies. These awards are presented annually by the King of Sweden on April 24.

The society also publishes *Kritisk Etnografi* (Critical Ethnography), a biannual, peer-reviewed, open-access journal launched in 2018, focusing on anthropology and ethnography in collaboration with Uppsala University's DiVA archive.

A spaced seed is a sequence alignment technique that allows for flexible matching of nucleic acid sequences by permitting certain base pair mismatches without penalty. This approach enhances the sensitivity of alignment algorithms, enabling the identification of biologically relevant similarities even when sequences are not identical. Spaced seeds have been shown to improve the speed and memory efficiency of homology searches compared to traditional non-spaced models, such as those used in BLAST. The concept has evolved over time, with significant contributions from various algorithms, including PatternHunter, which popularized the use of multiple spaced seeds for improved alignment accuracy.

Denshaw is a village in the civil parish of Saddleworth, Greater Manchester, England. Located on elevated Pennine terrain near the River Tame, it lies 11.4 miles northeast of Manchester city centre. With a population of around 500, Denshaw has a history dating back to the Stone Age and Bronze Age, evidenced by archaeological finds. Unlike neighboring industrial towns, it remained largely rural, though it housed a calico printing factory in the 19th century. The village includes historic structures such as Christ Church (1863) and the Junction Inn (1795). Denshaw is known for its annual Whit Friday brass band contest and has been referenced in music and media. It is governed by Oldham Metropolitan Borough Council and lies within the Oldham East and Saddleworth parliamentary constituency. Public transport is limited, with bus services connecting it to nearby towns.

Lu Rongjing (born August 1933) is a Chinese politician who served as Communist Party Secretary of Anhui (1988–1996) and Governor of Anhui (1988–1989). He later chaired the Anhui Provincial Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (1996–2000). Beginning his career in the Tongguanshan Copper Mine, Lu rose through provincial leadership roles, including deputy party secretary of Tongling and Ma'anshan, before assuming top positions in Anhui's government and party apparatus.

Juan Jesús Gutiérrez Robles, known as Juanito (born 17 February 1980), is a Spanish former professional footballer who played as a defensive midfielder. He began his career with Málaga CF, later representing clubs including Alavés, Real Sociedad, Almería, and Asteras Tripolis. Notably, he helped Almería reach the Copa del Rey semi-finals in 2011. Post-retirement, he served as Almería's assistant manager.

Celanese Corporation is a global leader in the chemicals and advanced materials industry, founded in 1918 by Swiss chemist Camille Dreyfus in New York City. Headquartered in Irving, Texas, the company specializes in producing a wide range of products, including acetyl intermediates, advanced engineered materials, and consumer specialties. Celanese is particularly known for its cellulose acetate, used in applications such as cigarette filters and fashion apparel. The company has undergone significant transformations, including mergers and acquisitions, and is publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol "CE." As of 2021, Celanese reported revenues of $8.54 billion and employed over 8,500 people.

Robbie Thorpe is an Aboriginal activist from the Krautungalung people of the Gunnai Nation, known for his advocacy on Indigenous rights, including the Pay The Rent initiative, the Aboriginal Passport initiative, and campaigns addressing genocide and sovereignty. A prominent figure since the 1970s, he founded *The Koorier* publications and initiated 3CR's *Fire First* radio program. Thorpe's activism extends to legal challenges, media production, and creative projects, with his story featured in the upcoming documentary *Our Warrior: The Story of Robbie Thorpe*.
Scrabble variants include Anagrab, Clabbers, Duplicate Scrabble, Ecological Scrabble, Solitaire Scrabble, Speed Scrabble, and more.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is an instrumental piece composed by Nino Rota for the 1972 film *The Godfather*. Originally written for the 1958 Italian film *Fortunella*, it gained fame as the film's romantic theme. The song, also known as *"Speak Softly Love"* with English lyrics by Larry Kusik, has been recorded in multiple languages. Though Rota's score was initially disqualified from an Academy Award due to its prior use, it became a chart success, with versions by Roger Williams, Andy Williams, and Al Martino reaching international popularity.

Messier 83 (also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy or NGC 5236) is a barred spiral galaxy located approximately 14.7 million light-years away in the constellation Hydra. Classified as SAB(s)c, it features loosely wound spiral arms and a weak central bar. M83 is a grand design spiral galaxy with active star formation, particularly along its spiral arms. It has hosted six observed supernovae and is the central galaxy of a subgroup within the Centaurus A/M83 Group. The Galaxy Evolution Explorer mission discovered young stars in its outer regions, challenging previous assumptions about star formation in such areas.

Emerich Vogl (12 August 1905 – 29 October 1971) was a Romanian footballer and manager who played as a defender and midfielder. He began his career at Chinezul Timișoara and later joined Juventus București, winning multiple league titles. Vogl represented Romania in two FIFA World Cups and managed both Juventus București and the national team several times after his playing career.

*Going Blank Again* is the second studio album by English shoegaze band Ride, released on 9 March 1992. Produced by Alan Moulder and the band, it features the singles "Leave Them All Behind" and "Twisterella." The album expanded on their debut's sound, blending shoegaze with psychedelic rock and power-pop influences. Critics praised its layered vocals and atmospheric guitars, and it has since been regarded as a key shoegaze release.

Bryant Grinder is an American machine tool manufacturer specializing in precision grinding machines, founded in 1909 by William LeRoy Bryant in Springfield, Vermont. Initially producing grinders for the automotive and aerospace industries, the company became a key supplier during both World Wars, particularly for aircraft engine components. Acquired by Ex-Cell-O in 1958 and later by Textron in 1986, Bryant expanded into CNC grinding and computer disc-drive technology. After financial struggles in the early 2000s, Vermont Machine Tool revived the company before Fives acquired its intellectual property in 2015. Bryant Grinder remains known for its high-precision grinding machines, now developed under Fives Landis Corp.

The Insurance Act, 1938 is an Indian law that consolidates and amends regulations governing the insurance business, establishing a framework for both domestic and foreign insurance companies operating in India.

The 1986 New York Mets season was one of the most memorable in franchise history, culminating in a World Series championship. Managed by Davey Johnson, the Mets finished the regular season with a National League-best record of 108–54, winning the National League East Division by 21+1⁄2 games. Key players included Darryl Strawberry, Dwight Gooden, Keith Hernandez, and Gary Carter, complemented by acquisitions like Tim Teufel and Bob Ojeda.

The season was marked by dominant play and memorable moments, including a come-from-behind victory in Game 6 of the World Series against the Boston Red Sox, highlighted by Mookie Wilson's ground ball that went through Bill Buckner's legs. The Mets defeated the Houston Astros in the NLCS. The season is chronicled in the ESPN 30 for 30 documentary "Once Upon a Time in Queens."

The olfactory tract is a key component of the olfactory system located in the brain, specifically within the olfactory sulcus on the inferior surface of the frontal lobes. It consists of medial and lateral striae and is involved in transmitting sensory information related to smell. Damage to the olfactory tract can lead to anosmia, the loss of the ability to smell.

Jasmina Obradović is a Serbian economist and politician who served in the Novi Sad city assembly (2012–2016) and the National Assembly of Serbia (2012–present) as a member of the Progressive Party. She has held multiple committee roles and led parliamentary friendship groups.
Douglas "Doug" Chapman is a Canadian stunt performer, stunt coordinator, actor, and stunt double for actors such as Kurt Russell and Christian Slater.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) in Seneca County, New York, located within the town of Seneca Falls. Originally incorporated as a village in 1831, it was dissolved on December 31, 2011, following a vote by residents. The area, historically inhabited by the Cayuga tribe, played a significant role in the Women's Rights Movement, hosting the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first women's rights convention in the United States. The village was also a hub for early industrial development, utilizing water power from the Seneca River. Today, Seneca Falls is home to the Women's Rights National Historical Park and the National Women's Hall of Fame.

Sir William Pole (1561–1635) was an English antiquary, historian, and politician from Devon. Baptized in Colyton, he served as Sheriff of Devon, MP for Bossiney, and was knighted by King James I in 1606. Pole is best known for his extensive unpublished manuscripts on Devon's history, genealogy, and heraldry, which influenced later historians.

"Ambush" is the fourth-season premiere of the American medical drama *ER*. Originally airing on September 25, 1997, the episode was directed by Thomas Schlamme and written by Carol Flint. Filmed live twice for East and West Coast broadcasts, it follows the staff of County General Hospital as they are documented by a PBS film crew. Key events include Dr. David Morgenstern suffering a heart attack, new surgeon Elizabeth Corday (Alex Kingston) joining the team, and John Carter learning he must repeat his internship. The episode received mixed reviews, with praise for its technical execution but criticism for its script. It drew 42.71 million viewers, becoming the most-watched drama season premiere in television history at the time. At the 50th Primetime Emmy Awards, it won for Outstanding Technical Direction and received additional nominations.

Bertil Stjernfelt (24 July 1917 – 21 January 2017) was a Swedish Navy lieutenant colonel and military historian. He served in coastal artillery, commanded Sweden's UN peacekeeping contingent in Cyprus (1966–1967), and later headed the Naval Staff's Intelligence Department. A prolific writer, he authored works on military history, including *Alarm i Atlantvallen*.

Climate change in The Gambia poses significant threats to the environment and economy, exacerbating issues such as droughts, floods, and coastal erosion. Agriculture, which constitutes 26% of the GDP and employs 68% of the workforce, is particularly vulnerable due to reliance on rain-fed systems. The government has initiated a Climate Change Priority Action Plan and collaborates with international organizations to address these challenges.

Dessie Glynn (7 June 1928 – 6 January 2017) was an Irish footballer who played as a forward for Drumcondra and Shelbourne in the League of Ireland. A prolific goalscorer, he helped Drumcondra win the league title in his first season (1949–50) and later earned an FAI Cup winner's medal in 1954. Glynn was the League of Ireland's top scorer in 1950–51 with 20 goals and netted 96 league goals for Drumcondra. He also earned two caps for the Republic of Ireland, scoring the winning goal on his debut against West Germany in 1951. After leaving Drumcondra in 1956, he played two seasons with Shelbourne before retiring in 1958.

FESTAC 77, officially known as the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture, was a significant cultural event held in Lagos, Nigeria, from January 15 to February 12, 1977. It was inspired by earlier movements promoting black culture, such as Négritude and Pan-Africanism, and followed the First World Festival of Black Arts in Dakar, Senegal, in 1966. Originally scheduled for 1970, the festival was postponed due to Nigeria's Civil War and subsequent political changes. 

FESTAC 77 featured a diverse array of performances, including music, dance, drama, and art exhibitions, showcasing the talents of participants from 59 countries. The festival's opening ceremony included a grand parade and symbolic acts of unity. A central colloquium addressed issues of intellectual freedom and cultural identity among black and African artists. The event also included traditional celebrations like the Durbar and Regatta festivals. Following its conclusion, the artifacts and cultural contributions were preserved, leading to the establishment of the Center for Black and African Arts and Civilization, which continues to honor the legacy of FESTAC 77.

The Schillinger System of Musical Composition, developed by Joseph Schillinger, is a comprehensive approach to music theory that integrates scientific analysis with musical creation. Schillinger, a professor at The New School in New York City, taught notable musicians including George Gershwin and Glenn Miller. His system aimed to liberate composers from traditional constraints by applying mathematical principles to music, although it has been criticized for its complexity and uneven tone. Despite skepticism during his lifetime, Schillinger's methods influenced jazz education and were incorporated into curricula at institutions like Berklee College of Music, where his legacy continues to impact contemporary music composition.

The Gao Empire, established around the 9th century and lasting until 1430, was a significant political entity in West Africa, centered in the town of Gao along the Niger River. Initially a hub for fishing and trade, Gao evolved into a kingdom that played a crucial role in regional commerce and manufacturing, particularly in crafting valued carnelian beads. The empire's early history is primarily documented through external Arabic sources, as indigenous written records are scarce. By the 10th century, Gao had embraced Islam, with the conversion of its ruling dynasty occurring by the end of the 11th century. The empire experienced a decline in the late 13th century, becoming part of the Mali Empire, before regaining independence under the Sunni dynasty in the 15th century. The Gao Empire ultimately laid the groundwork for the rise of the Songhai Empire, which expanded significantly under leaders like Sunni Ali Ber. Today, the historical legacy of the Gao Empire is part of modern Mali's cultural heritage.

Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid is a Supreme Court case decided on April 1, 2021, concerning the definition of an "automatic telephone dialing system" (ATDS) under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) of 1991. The case arose when Noah Duguid received unsolicited text messages from Facebook despite not having an account. The Court ruled that to qualify as an ATDS, a device must use a random or sequential number generator, thereby narrowing the scope of the TCPA and impacting telemarketing regulations.

Royalty Exchange, founded in 2011 in Raleigh, North Carolina, is an online marketplace for buying and selling music royalties. The company was established by Sean Peace, Reggie Calloway, and Wilson Owens, and later acquired by private investors in 2015, relocating to Denver, Colorado. In 2017, it launched Royalty Flow to acquire and hold royalty streams from major artists, including a notable attempt to acquire a share of Eminem's catalog. Royalty Exchange also offers Private Syndicate investments, allowing accredited investors to buy shares in music catalogs. The company became a member of Techstars Music Accelerator in 2017.
Babócsa is a village in Somogy county, Hungary, near Croatia.

Kogiidae is a family of small cetaceans, commonly known as kogiids, that includes the dwarf sperm whale (Kogia sima) and the pygmy sperm whale (Kogia breviceps). These whales are characterized by their short rostrums, high levels of asymmetry, and unique sound production organs. Kogiidae are believed to inhabit all oceans except polar regions and primarily feed on squid using suction feeding techniques. Their populations face threats from fishing activities and plastic pollution.

The karbovanets was a currency of Ukraine in three distinct periods. The first karbovanets was introduced by the Ukrainian People's Republic in 1917. A second karbovanets was issued by the German occupying government during World War II (1942–1945). The third karbovanets (ISO 4217 code: UAK) replaced the Soviet ruble in 1992 and was itself replaced by the hryvnia in 1996 due to hyperinflation, at a rate of 100,000 karbovantsiv to 1 hryvnia.

"Jam" is a song by American singer Michael Jackson, released on July 13, 1992, as the lead single from his eighth studio album, *Dangerous*. The track, which features a blend of new jack swing and industrial funk, was co-written and produced by Jackson alongside Teddy Riley, René Moore, and Bruce Swedien. "Jam" received positive reviews from music critics, who praised its energetic production and Jackson's vocal performance, highlighting its themes of music as an escape from worldly issues. The accompanying music video, directed by David Kellogg, showcases Jackson teaching basketball legend Michael Jordan to dance while Jordan teaches Jackson basketball skills, featuring special effects and cameos from rap groups Kris Kross and Naughty by Nature. "Jam" was performed as the opening number during Jackson's Dangerous World Tour and was included in various compilations, though it was not part of the final setlist for the canceled *This Is It* concert series.

John Pierrakos (February 8, 1921 – February 1, 2001) was a Greek-American psychiatrist and co-founder of bioenergetic analysis, a body-oriented psychotherapy. A student of Wilhelm Reich, he collaborated with Alexander Lowen to develop bioenergetics, emphasizing grounding and energetic release. Later, he diverged from Lowen, integrating spiritual concepts into therapy and founding the Institute of Core Energetics in 1973. His work combined physical, emotional, and spiritual healing, focusing on the body's energy systems.

Yossi Gross (born February 5, 1947) is a Hungarian-born Israeli entrepreneur, inventor, and engineer. He is known for founding numerous medical device companies and for his work in drug delivery systems.

Gross immigrated to Israel with his parents at the age of three and later earned an MSc in aeronautical engineering from the Technion Israel Institute of Technology. He worked at Israel Aircraft Industries before starting his entrepreneurial career. Gross's early inventions included small electric consumer appliances. He then transitioned to medical devices, developing drug delivery systems in a joint venture with Elan Corporation.

From the late 1990s, Gross founded 27 medical device companies, attracting investment from various venture capital firms and corporations. In 2007, he launched Rainbow Medical, a venture fund and incubator that supports his technology ideas. Rainbow Medical focuses on early-stage startups in various medical fields, including cardiovascular, diabetes, ophthalmology, and aesthetics. Gross currently serves as the CTO of Rainbow Medical.

Panhard was a French automobile and military vehicle manufacturer founded in 1887 by René Panhard and Émile Levassor. Initially known as Panhard et Levassor, the company pioneered automotive innovations, including the modern transmission and the standard car layout. It produced passenger cars until 1967, after which it focused solely on military vehicles. Acquired by Citroën and later Renault Trucks Defense, Panhard ceased operations in 2018. Its legacy includes the Panhard rod suspension system.

Robert Altman (1944 – September 24, 2021) was an American photographer renowned for his iconic images capturing the essence of the 1960s counterculture. His work documented the vibrancy of the era, including the Acid culture, Summer of Love, and the Hippie Movement, with a focus on themes of freedom, protest, and revolutionary ideologies. Altman’s photography, influenced by Ansel Adams, is celebrated for its raw, natural style and unique compositional perspective, which vividly portrayed the connection between performers and their audiences during events like Woodstock.

Altman’s photographs have been widely exhibited, including at Abbey Road Studios, The Beat Museum, and the Newseum, and are part of the permanent collections of institutions such as the Library of Congress, the Smithsonian Institution, and the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. His work has been featured in numerous publications, and his book *The Sixties: Photographs* (2007) remains a definitive visual record of the decade.

In 2009, Altman collaborated with Macy's Herald Square in New York for the "Art Under Glass" series, showcasing 175 of his images. He was awarded an honorary Doctor of Arts degree from Digital Media Arts College in 2010. Altman passed away on September 24, 2021, from esophageal cancer, leaving behind a legacy as one of the foremost chroniclers of 1960s culture.
Léonie d'Aunet (1820–1879) was a French author and playwright, known for her Arctic travelogue and her affair with Victor Hugo.

Jean Messiha (born Hossam Boutros Messiha; September 10, 1970) is a French economist, civil servant, and political commentator. Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a Coptic Christian family, he became a naturalized French citizen at age 20.

Messiha graduated from Sciences Po and the École nationale d'administration, beginning his career as a high-ranking civil servant in 2005. He became an advisor to Marine Le Pen in 2014 and spokesman for the "Horaces," a group supporting the National Rally. He unsuccessfully ran for the National Assembly in 2017.

After leaving the National Rally in 2020, Messiha joined Éric Zemmour's Reconquête party in 2022. He is known for his controversial views on immigration and Islam, and his Twitter account was permanently suspended for violating hateful conduct policies.

Queen's Fort Military Museum, located in Bloemfontein, South Africa, is a military museum established in 1972, originally serving as a fort built after the Battle of Boomplaats in 1848. Initially named in honor of Queen Victoria, the fort underwent several reconstructions and served various military purposes over the years. The museum showcases exhibitions related to major military conflicts in the Free State and the struggle against apartheid.
Willard Gallery was an influential art gallery in New York City, founded by Marian Guthrie Willard in 1940 and active until its closure in 1987.
Felix Johannes Oinas (1893-2004) was an Estonian folklorist and linguist who emigrated to the United States after World War II.
Ward No. 8 is one of the 144 wards of the Kolkata Municipal Corporation in West Bengal, India. It covers the Bagbazar and Shobhabazar neighborhoods and has a population of 18,760 as of 2011.

The Treaty of Worms, signed in 1743, involved significant territorial and financial agreements between Maria Theresa and the Kingdom of Sardinia. Under the treaty, Maria Theresa ceded various territories, including parts of Piacenza and claims to the marquisate of Finale, while Great Britain agreed to pay £300,000 for the cession and provide an annual subsidy of £200,000. A secret convention also promised Maria Theresa an additional annual subsidy of £300,000. The treaty faced criticism for its unjust terms regarding the marquisate of Finale, which had previously been guaranteed to Genoa.

VirusTotal is a website dedicated to analyzing files and URLs for potential malware and other security threats. Launched in June 2004, it is currently owned by Chronicle, a subsidiary of Google LLC. VirusTotal aggregates results from numerous antivirus engines, website scanning engines, and file characterization tools to provide users with a comprehensive analysis of uploaded files or submitted URLs.

The service is available in multiple languages and offers optional registration. Users can submit files and URLs through the website or via various tools, including a Windows Uploader (discontinued in 2017), uploaders for Mac OS X and Linux, browser extensions, and a mobile app for Android. VirusTotal also provides a public API that allows developers to automate scans and access reports.

Files uploaded to VirusTotal are stored and may be shared with anti-malware companies to improve detection rates. The website offers a range of antivirus products, website/domain scanning engines, and file characterization tools and datasets.

Abha Dawesar (born January 1, 1974) is an Indian novelist and artist based in New York City, known for her award-winning novel "Babyji" (2005) and other works such as "Family Values" and "That Summer in Paris." A Harvard graduate, she also explores themes of digital technology and social behavior.

Dan Carpenter (born November 25, 1985) is a former American football placekicker who played in the NFL for the Miami Dolphins, Arizona Cardinals, New York Jets, and Buffalo Bills. A standout at the University of Montana, he went undrafted in 2008 but achieved Pro Bowl status in 2009. Carpenter is known for his accuracy, setting a rookie record for consecutive field goals with the Dolphins and earning multiple AFC Special Teams Player of the Week honors during his career.

Gold Hill is an unincorporated community in Lee County, Alabama, United States. Settled in the 1830s, it was named for an early settler, Goldsmith. The community was also known as Gold Ridge due to a postal mix-up. Gold Hill is home to "Roamer's Roost", the residence of William P. Spratling and his son.
Soter Ortynsky (1866–1916) was the first bishop of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church in the United States, known for establishing orphanages, a cathedral, and fostering church independence.

Pieter Woortman (23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a German-born colonial administrator who served as the Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast from 1767 to 1780. Initially a soldier with the Dutch West India Company, he later established a profitable slave trade and had significant influence through his relationships with both European and local communities.

*The Technicolor Time Machine* is a 1967 science fiction novel by Harry Harrison. The plot follows Barney Hendrickson, a mediocre film director who uses a time machine to film a Viking movie in the past. The movie becomes a smash hit, and the film crew inadvertently influences history.

Henrique Dumont (July 20, 1832 – August 30, 1892) was a Brazilian engineer and coffee planter, known as the "Coffee King" for owning one of Brazil's largest coffee farms. A graduate of École Centrale Paris, he modernized coffee production with railroads and machinery. He was the father of aviation pioneer Alberto Santos Dumont.

Radnor and Lee is an American indie folk duo formed in 2016 by actor-musician Josh Radnor and singer-songwriter Ben Lee. Known for their spiritually reflective lyrics and acoustic sound, they have released two albums: *Radnor and Lee* (2017) and *Golden State* (2020).

Melchior Inchofer (1585 – September 28, 1648) was a Jesuit theologian and writer known for his involvement in the examination of Galileo's *Dialogue of the Two World Systems*. Born to Lutheran parents in Kőszeg, he converted to Catholicism and joined the Society of Jesus in 1607. Inchofer taught philosophy, mathematics, and theology in Sicily before moving to Rome, where he became a political adviser and contributed to various theological works. His notable publications include *Tractus Syllepticus* and *Annales ecclesiastici regni Hungariae*, the latter pioneering the science of sources in ecclesiastical history. Inchofer's later years were marked by controversy and a trial by his order, leading to his death in Milan.

The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a non-departmental public body created in 1995. In 2007, it merged with PPARC to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council.
Ham Green Pottery refers to a historical pottery site known for its hand-thrown jugs, dating from the 12th century, characterized by distinctive glazes and decorative patterns.

*Anima Yell!* (アニマエール!, *Anima Ēru!*) is a Japanese four-panel manga series written and illustrated by Tsukasa Unohana. It was serialized in *Manga Time Kirara Carat* from February 2016 to August 2020, with five tankōbon volumes published. The story follows Kohane Hatoya, a high school girl who starts a cheerleading club. An anime adaptation by Doga Kobo aired from October to December 2018, running for 12 episodes. The series blends slice-of-life and sports elements, receiving mixed reviews for its characters and humor but praise for its animation and charm. Characters also appear in the mobile game *Kirara Fantasia*.

*Majha Hoshil Na* is an Indian Marathi-language television drama series that aired on Zee Marathi from 2 March 2020 to 28 August 2021. Produced by Ocean Films Company and directed by Aniket Sane, the show follows the love story of Sai, a wealthy woman, and Aditya, a middle-class man, as they navigate societal and familial challenges before and after their marriage. The series, set in Mumbai and other locations like Dapoli and Manali, gained popularity for its engaging storyline and became the top-rated Marathi show in 2020.

Pridwen is the legendary shield of King Arthur, first appearing in Geoffrey of Monmouth's 12th-century *Historia Regum Britanniae*. Its name, possibly meaning "white face" or "blessed form," derives from Welsh tradition, where *Prydwen* was Arthur's ship. Geoffrey describes Pridwen as bearing an image of the Virgin Mary, a motif later expanded in medieval texts like Wace's *Roman de Brut* and Layamon's *Brut*, which depict the shield with intricate religious symbolism. The shield also appears in works such as *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*, reinforcing its Arthurian legacy.

Zeitenspiegel is a German journalism agency founded in 1976 by Uli Reinhardt and headquartered in Weinstadt, Germany. Specializing in print and news publishing, the agency reported a revenue of €1.7 million in 2008. Zeitenspiegel operates the Zeitenspiegel-Reportageschule Günter Dahl, a journalism academy that offers one-year courses to train aspiring feature journalists. The academy, taught by Zeitenspiegel members and renowned professionals, has been recognized as one of the top journalism schools in Germany.

Since 1998, Zeitenspiegel has awarded the Hansel Mieth Prize, honoring its honorary member Johanna "Hansel" Mieth, for outstanding reporting on socially relevant topics. The prize is given to a reporter-photographer team, while the Gabriel Grüner Grant supports emerging journalists in researching promising stories. Both awards reflect Zeitenspiegel's commitment to high-quality, socially conscious journalism.

Louis of Brienne, also known as Louis of Acre, was the second son of John of Brienne and Berengaria of León. Through his marriage to Agnes of Beaumont, he became the Viscount of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe and a vassal of Charles of Anjou. Raised at the French royal court, he participated in King Louis IX's crusade against Egypt and maintained close ties with the Castilian royal court.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a 1986 single by the English band New Order, featured on their fourth studio album, *Brotherhood*. Written by band members Gillian Gilbert, Peter Hook, Stephen Morris, and Bernard Sumner, the song is a hallmark of the new wave, synth-pop, and alternative dance genres. The single, released on November 5, 1986, gained acclaim for its innovative use of electronic instrumentation, including the Fairlight CMI music workstation, and its layered production. A remixed 12-inch version by Shep Pettibone appears on the compilation *Substance*, while other versions feature on subsequent compilations like *Best Of* and *Singles*.

The music video, directed by Robert Longo, incorporates imagery inspired by his "Men in the Cities" lithographs and features surreal scenes of people in business suits flying through the air. Critically lauded as a "synth-pop masterpiece," the song has been ranked among the greatest of all time by *Rolling Stone*, *Billboard*, and other publications, cementing its legacy in New Order's discography.

Randy Kaplan is an American singer-songwriter, actor, and playwright known for his eclectic musical style that blends American roots, country blues, and comedic storytelling. Born and raised in Dix Hills, Long Island, Kaplan studied philosophy, writing, and literature at the University of Michigan and UCLA before pursuing acting and music. He began his career performing in theater, film, and television, including appearances on *Beauty and the Beast* and *Growing Pains*. In the early 1990s, he fronted the band "i" in Los Angeles before relocating to New York City, where he released his debut album, *Boyish Hips*, in 1997.

Kaplan is best known for his "not-JUST-for-kids" music, which has earned critical acclaim and numerous awards, including recognition from NPR, People magazine, and Nickelodeon Parents' Picks. His family-friendly albums, such as *Loquat Rooftop* and *Trippin' Round the Mitten*, feature a mix of original songs, reimagined classics, and humorous storytelling. Kaplan has also collaborated with bluegrass musician Mike West and contributed to compilations like Putumayo's *American Playground*. In addition to his music career, Kaplan has published poetry and co-created the musical comedy *Cleave*. He currently resides in a Detroit suburb with his wife and son.

The Kuban River is a river in Eastern Europe, in Russia. It originates on the slopes of Mount Elbrus and flows 870 kilometers (540 mi) to the Sea of Azov. The Kuban's basin size is 57,900 square kilometers (22,400 sq mi). It is primarily fed by glaciers and snow. The river does not freeze over. It is characterized by frequent floods. The Kuban River delta is a Ramsar Wetland. The river flows through mixed forests, submediterranean forests and steppe landscapes. Its delta is rich in plankton and benthos, supporting diverse fish and bird populations. The river is used for navigation and powers the Krasnodar Thermal Power Plant. The Kuban Cossacks settled in its basin in the 18th–19th centuries.

Phenoxyethanol is a colorless, oily liquid with germicidal and preservative properties, widely used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and organic synthesis. It is produced via hydroxyethylation of phenol and is effective against bacteria and yeast.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The team was led by head coach Matt Luke in his first season. Ole Miss played as a member of the Southeastern Conference (SEC).

The season followed significant coaching staff changes after 2016, including the hiring of Wesley McGriff as defensive coordinator. The program was also under NCAA investigation, facing 21 charges, including 15 Level I violations. Ole Miss imposed self-penalties, including a 2017 post-season ban and scholarship reductions.

The Rebels' 2017 schedule, announced in September 2016, featured 7 home and 5 away games. Key SEC matchups included home games against Arkansas, LSU, Texas A&M, and Vanderbilt, and road games against Alabama, Auburn, Kentucky, and Mississippi State. The team's final record was 6-6 (3-5 SEC).

**Tennessee State Route 100** (SR 100) is a primary and secondary state route in West, Middle, and Southwest Tennessee. It runs for 158.31 miles (254.78 km) from US 64 in Whiteville to US 70S in Nashville.

An eruption column is a cloud of hot volcanic ash, tephra, and gases emitted during an explosive volcanic eruption. The ash and tephra are ejected at high speeds and can rise several kilometers into the atmosphere due to convection. The height of the column depends on factors like vent diameter, gas content, and atmospheric conditions.

Eruption columns pose significant hazards. If a column becomes too dense, it can collapse, creating dangerous pyroclastic flows. Volcanic ash can also severely damage aircraft, as demonstrated by several incidents where planes encountered eruption columns, leading to engine failure and other problems. Volcanic Ash Advisory Centers monitor eruption columns to mitigate these risks. Large eruptions can inject material into the stratosphere, affecting global temperatures.

Khan Bahadur Nassakh (1833–1889), born Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr, was a Bengali Muslim poet, government officer, and scholar of Urdu and Persian. He served as a deputy magistrate in the Bengal Presidency and was known for his contributions to Urdu literature, including works like *Daftar-e-Bemisal* and *Sukhan-e-Shuara*. Nassakh also translated Persian poetry and critiqued notable poets, earning praise from figures like Ghalib.
Cheick Oumar Dabo (born 1981) is a Malian former footballer who played as a striker.

Hardoi is a village in Raebareli district, Uttar Pradesh, India. As of 2011, its population is 2,308, in 440 households, and its area is 5.264 km<sup>2</sup>. It was historically ruled by the Bhars, who were then subjugated by the Jaunpur Sultanate in the 15th century.

The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in 2003 in Indianapolis, consisting of lead singer and guitarist Irwin Sparkes and drummer Alan Sharland. The band's name is derived from the colloquial term for residents of Indiana. After returning to London, they signed with RCA and released their debut album, *The Trick to Life*, in 2007, featuring the hit single "Goodbye Mr A." Following their second album, *The Illusion of Safety*, the band departed from Sony in 2011 due to promotional disputes. They later released *The News from Nowhere* in 2014 and *The Secret Service* in 2015. In 2021, they collaborated on the charity single "Route 66" for the Euro 2020 Football Championship, which topped the Official Singles Sales Chart.

The House of Zogu is an Albanian royal family founded in 1928 by King Zog I, who ruled until Italy's 1939 invasion. The current head is Crown Prince Leka II, the only living male descendant of Zog I. The heir presumptive is Skënder Zogu, his first cousin once removed.

TDRS-9, originally designated TDRS-I, is a NASA communications satellite launched on March 8, 2002, aboard an Atlas IIA rocket from Cape Canaveral. Part of the Tracking and Data Relay Satellite System, it experienced significant delays in reaching geostationary orbit due to a fuel tank pressurization issue caused by a wiring error. Despite these challenges, TDRS-9 became fully operational and has exceeded its planned 11-year mission lifespan.

The Jamaica Premier League is the top professional football league in Jamaica. Founded in 1973, it is contested by 12 clubs. The league has produced four Caribbean champions, with Portmore United winning the most championships (7 titles). Cavalier F.C. are the current champions (2021). The league season typically runs from August to May.

The 17th Arizona State Legislature was the state legislature that convened in Phoenix, Arizona from January 1, 1945, to December 31, 1946. The legislature met for its regular session from January 18 to March 9, 1945. The body was composed of 19 senators and 58 representatives, with the Democratic Party controlling both chambers. The Legislature also held three special sessions, the first of which was held from September 10 through September 29, 1945.

Lead Section:  

*MercyMe, It's Christmas!* (2015) is the first Christmas album by contemporary Christian band MercyMe. Featuring a mix of original songs and holiday classics, it received widespread critical acclaim for its festive and uplifting sound.

Lake Temescal is a reservoir located in Oakland, California, created in 1868 by damming Temescal Creek to provide drinking water for the East Bay. Once a sag pond along the Hayward Fault, it became a public park in 1936 under the East Bay Regional Park District. Today, the lake features recreational amenities, including a beach, fishing, and trails, and receives around 200,000 visitors annually. It faces challenges such as sedimentation, blue-green algae outbreaks, and earthquake-related risks due to its location on the Hayward Fault.

The Vidarbha movement is a political campaign advocating for the creation of a separate state of Vidarbha from the eastern region of Maharashtra, India. The demand dates back to 1938, when the Central Provinces legislature passed a resolution supporting statehood. Despite recommendations from the Fazal Ali-led State Reorganisation Commission (1956), Vidarbha was merged with Maharashtra in 1960 under the Nagpur Pact, which promised equitable development. However, perceived neglect and economic disparities have fueled ongoing demands, supported by various political parties, including the BJP and regional groups. The movement remains a contentious issue in Maharashtra's politics.
Phlebiella is a genus of fungi in the order Polyporales, lacking cystidia.
1992 Taiwanese legislative election was held on 19 December, with the Kuomintang winning 95 of 161 seats.

Carman George Blough (1895–1981) was an American accountant and educator. He served as the first Chief Accountant of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (1934–1938) and later worked at Arthur Andersen & Co. A professor at Columbia University (1947–1961), he was a key figure in accounting standards and regulation.

"Wild Wild Life" is a 1986 song by Talking Heads from their album *True Stories*. A synth-rock track, it was praised for its quirky, upbeat style. Its music video, featuring band members parodying celebrities, won "Best Group Video" at the 1987 MTV Video Music Awards. The song has been used in several films and trailers.

Courtney Andrew Walsh (born October 30, 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer and one of the greatest fast bowlers in the history of cricket. Representing the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, Walsh played 132 Test matches and 205 One Day Internationals (ODIs), taking 519 and 227 wickets, respectively. He was the first bowler in Test cricket history to surpass 500 wickets and held the record for the most Test wickets until 2004. Known for his remarkable durability, accuracy, and sportsmanship, Walsh formed a legendary bowling partnership with Curtly Ambrose, sharing 421 wickets in 49 Tests.

Walsh also had a distinguished domestic career, playing for Jamaica and Gloucestershire County Cricket Club. He was named one of Wisden's Cricketers of the Year in 1987 and was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010. Post-retirement, Walsh has served as a coach, including as head coach of the West Indies women's team. Renowned for his dedication and humility, Walsh remains a celebrated figure in cricket history.

Charles J. Givens (born in Decatur, Illinois – July 12, 1998) was an American entrepreneur, author, and financial advisor who rose to prominence in the late 1980s and 1990s. Despite a challenging upbringing, Givens built a multimillion-dollar empire and authored best-selling books such as *Wealth Without Risk* and *Financial Self-Defense*. At his peak, he led the Charles J. Givens Organization, which provided financial education to over 600,000 members and generated $104 million in revenue.

However, Givens' career was marred by controversy, including numerous lawsuits and fraud claims. He was ordered to refund $14.1 million in a California fraud case and settled other legal disputes. Givens died of prostate cancer on July 12, 1998.

St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum, is a Grade I listed Church of England parish church in Devon, England, dedicated to Saint Andrew. Originally medieval, it was extensively rebuilt in the late 15th century and restored in 1868–69. The church is notable for its Drake aisle monuments, including works by John Bacon Senior and Junior, and Richard Westmacott. It houses an organ installed in 1849, later expanded, and a peal of eight bells, five of which date to 1723.

A. J. Alan, born Leslie Harrison Lambert on November 11, 1883, was an English magician, intelligence officer, author, and radio broadcaster. He gained fame for his whimsical storytelling on the radio, becoming a popular figure in the 1920s and 1930s, while also serving in naval intelligence during both World Wars.

Yawalapití is an indigenous language spoken in the Xingu Indigenous Park of Brazil. In 2006, it was reported to have only 8 native speakers. It belongs to the Arawakan language family, within the Paresí–Xingu subgroup. Yawalapití shares a similar phonemic inventory with Waurá.
FOX Arena is an Australian subscription television channel owned by Foxtel Networks, originally launched on April 22, 1995, and rebranded on July 1, 2020.

Leslie Quirk (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004) was a Manx language activist and folklorist. A key figure in preserving the Manx language, he learned from the last native speakers and later taught new generations of learners. He worked with the Irish Folklore Commission and led the Manx Folk-Life Survey, documenting traditional Manx culture. Awarded the Reih Bleeaney Vanannan in 1997, Quirk played a vital role in the revival of Manx Gaelic.
Aberdare Girls' School was a secondary school in Aberdare, Wales, established in 1913 and closed in July 2014, known for its motto "You cannot reap unless you sow."
