Terry George (born 1965) is an English entrepreneur known for running gay nightclubs in Leeds. He and his husband featured in the documentary *Two Queens and a Castle*.

An institute is a type of organization, often focused on research, education, or professional standards. In the United Kingdom and the Isle of Man, the term is legally protected and may only be used by organizations conducting high-level research or representing professional bodies of the highest standing. Businesses must obtain approval from the Secretary of State before using "institute" in their name under the Business Names Act. Unauthorized use constitutes a criminal offence.

Rui Pinto (born Rui Pedro Gonçalves Pinto on October 20, 1988) is a Portuguese whistleblower and hacker known for founding the Football Leaks website, which exposed corruption and financial misconduct in the football industry. Pinto, a self-taught computer enthusiast, began his career in 2015 by acquiring confidential documents from football agencies, leading to significant investigations into tax evasion and financial irregularities involving high-profile players and clubs. He was arrested in January 2019 on charges of attempted extortion and illegally accessing information, and he was extradited to Portugal, where he faced 147 criminal charges. Pinto's trial began in September 2020, during which he maintained that he acted as a whistleblower. In April 2019, he received the European whistleblower award, recognizing his contributions to exposing corruption.

Ningali Lawford (1967 – 11 August 2019) was an Australian Aboriginal actress known for her roles in *Rabbit-Proof Fence* (2002) and *Last Cab to Darwin* (2015), the latter earning her an AACTA Award nomination. A Walmadjari woman, she gained acclaim for her one-woman show *Ningali*, which won awards at the Edinburgh Festival Fringe. She died while touring *The Secret River* in Scotland.

The Church of Nossa Senhora do Rosário is a Roman Catholic church in Topo, São Jorge, Azores, Portugal. Originally built in the 16th century, it was damaged by earthquakes in 1757 and 1980. Reconstructed in 1761, it features Baroque elements, including gilded retables and religious paintings.

The Taguig Integrated Terminal Exchange (TITX) is an upcoming intermodal transport terminal located in Arca South, Taguig, Philippines, projected to serve 160,000 passengers. The project, part of a public-private partnership, was awarded to Ayala Land in January 2016 after a bidding process that began in 2014. Although construction was initially set to start in September 2016, the groundbreaking ceremony took place in January 2018, with operations expected to commence by 2020. The project's estimated cost is P5.20 billion, though pre-construction activities have faced delays due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
**Enrico Morin** (1841–1910) was an Italian admiral and politician who served as minister of the Navy.

CCM, originally known as C.C.M., is a Canadian bicycle company founded in 1899 in Weston, Ontario. It emerged from the merger of four major manufacturers and quickly became the leading bicycle producer in Canada, accounting for 85% of the market. After facing financial difficulties, CCM declared bankruptcy in 1983 and was acquired by Procycle Group Inc., which continues to produce CCM-branded bicycles today.

2011 MD is a small near-Earth asteroid, approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in diameter, discovered on June 22, 2011, by the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research (LINEAR) program. Initially mistaken for space debris, it was later confirmed as an asteroid. During its close approach to Earth on June 27, 2011, it passed within 0.1 lunar distances, altering its orbital period. Observations revealed it to be a porous rubble pile with a density similar to water. The asteroid posed no threat to Earth, as it would have burned up in the atmosphere if it entered. In 2014, NASA identified 2011 MD as a potential candidate for the Asteroid Redirect Mission.

Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication used to treat relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. It is a proteasome inhibitor that irreversibly binds to and blocks the activity of the 20S proteasome, leading to the accumulation of damaged proteins and cancer cell death. Developed from the natural compound epoxomicin, carfilzomib was first studied by Craig Crews at Yale University and later advanced by Proteolix, Inc., which was acquired by Onyx Pharmaceuticals.  

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved carfilzomib in 2012 based on clinical trials showing efficacy in heavily pretreated patients. Common side effects include thrombocytopenia, anemia, and fatigue. It is administered intravenously and is often used in combination with lenalidomide and dexamethasone. The ASPIRE trial demonstrated improved progression-free survival with this combination compared to standard therapy.
Hamiota Municipality, located in Manitoba, Canada, was incorporated on January 1, 2015.

Lis Shoshi (born June 29, 1994) is a Kosovan professional basketball player who plays as a center and is currently a free agent. A member of the Kosovo national basketball team, he has played for various teams across Europe and Israel, including Sigal Prishtina, Pieno žvaigždės, and Maccabi Ashdod. Shoshi won the 2018 Baltic League Championship with Pieno žvaigždės and has been recognized for his rebounding and shot-blocking abilities. He played college basketball at Howard College and Little Rock, where he led the Sun Belt Conference in blocks during his senior year.

Annette Krauss is a contemporary artist known for her collaborative projects that address social issues and cultural critique. Notable works include "Read the Masks. Tradition is Not Given," which challenges the racist Zwarte Piet tradition in the Netherlands, and "Hidden Curriculum," which explores unspoken educational practices with high school students. Krauss is also a co-founder of the cultural collective Read-in and has contributed to various artistic research initiatives.

*The Ladies of the House* is a 2014 American horror film directed by John Stuart Wildman. It follows three men who are invited into the home of a stripper and her roommates, only to discover that the women are cannibalistic and intend to kill them. The film premiered at the Dallas International Film Festival on April 4, 2014, and received generally positive reviews from horror-focused websites.

Transbaikal, also known as Dauria, is a mountainous region in eastern Russia, stretching from Lake Baikal to the Chinese and Mongolian borders. Historically inhabited by the Daur people, it was part of the Transbaikal Oblast in Imperial Russia and later the Far Eastern Republic. The region is known for its unique flora and fauna, including the Dahurian larch, and contains protected areas like the Dauria Nature Reserve.

Harry Kroger (1935 - September 2022) was an American physicist and electrical engineer. He received a Ph.D. in physics from Cornell University in 1962. Kroger held research and management positions at Sperry Research Center and MCC, and was a professor of electrical engineering at Binghamton University. He held multiple patents in superconductive and semiconducting technologies.

Artificial dielectrics are materials designed to mimic the electromagnetic properties of natural dielectric solids but are constructed from macroscopic elements. The term was coined in 1948 by Winston E. Kock at Bell Laboratories, motivated by the need for lightweight, low-loss materials in large devices.

Natural dielectrics respond to electromagnetic fields at the atomic or molecular level, resulting in macroscopic properties described by electric permittivity and magnetic permeability. To replicate this, artificial dielectrics use larger, more accessible scatterers arranged in a lattice structure, responding to electromagnetic fields in a manner similar to atoms in natural materials. This approach is particularly effective at radio and microwave frequencies, where the wavelength of the applied field is significantly larger than the spacing between the artificial atoms.

The design of these artificial dielectrics involves carefully selecting the geometric shapes of the scattering elements, such as spheres, disks, or conducting strips, to achieve the desired electromagnetic response. Examples of artificial dielectrics include rodded mediums, also known as wire meshes or wire grids, consisting of square lattices of thin, parallel wires. Artificial dielectrics are considered a precursor to metamaterials.

Comproportionation is a chemical reaction where two species of the same element in different oxidation states combine to form a single product in an intermediate state. Its likelihood can be predicted using Frost diagrams.

A temporary crown is a short-term dental restoration used to protect a prepared tooth while a permanent crown is being fabricated. It can be made from preformed plastic, composite, or metal shells, or custom-formed from acrylic or bis-acrylic resin. Temporary crowns maintain function, aesthetics, and gum health, and are cemented with provisional cement for easy removal. Proper oral hygiene is essential to prevent complications before the final crown is placed.

First Manhattan is a financial services firm founded in 1964 and headquartered in New York City, specializing in investment management for high-net-worth individuals and institutional clients. With over $16 billion in assets under management, it employs a long-term, value-oriented investment strategy, closely aligned with the principles of Warren Buffett.

Muhammad bin Saud Al Muqrin (1687–1765) was the founder of the First Saudi State and the Emir of Diriyah from 1727 to 1765. He formed a historic alliance with Islamic reformer Muhammad bin Abdul Wahhab, establishing the foundation for the modern Saudi state. His governance, based on Islamic principles, shaped the enduring structure of Saudi rule.

*Great Bliss, Vol. 1* is a 1991 jazz album by David S. Ware, released on Silkheart Records. It was recorded in New York in January 1990. The album features Ware's quartet, including pianist Matthew Shipp, bassist William Parker, and drummer Marc Edwards. AllMusic notes it as the launching pad for the quartet that made Ware's name.

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC) is a national high-performance computing centre in Ireland, established in 2005. It operates supercomputers such as Kay, named after Kathleen Antonelli, and provides services to researchers, government agencies, and industry.

John Zachary Young (18 March 1907 – 4 July 1997) was a British zoologist and neurophysiologist known for discovering the squid giant axon. A professor at University College London, his research on nerve function influenced Nobel Prize-winning work. He also wrote influential textbooks on vertebrates and mammals.

Chester Creek is a tributary of the Delaware River in southeastern Pennsylvania, formed by its East and West Branches. Historically significant, it hosted early paper mills and Swedish settlements, and it remains ecologically healthy, supporting native species like brook trout and freshwater eels.

Alfred Lubbock (31 October 1845 – 17 July 1916) was an English sportsman who played first-class cricket for Kent and the Marylebone Cricket Club (MCC) and appeared in the 1875 FA Cup Final for Old Etonians. A right-handed batsman, he played 28 first-class matches, scoring two centuries, and was considered one of the finest batsmen of his era. Educated at Eton, he captained the school cricket team and later played club cricket for sides like I Zingari. He worked as a Lloyd's underwriter and authored *Memories of Eton and Etonians* (1899).

The discography of Face to Face, an American punk rock band formed in 1991, includes 10 studio albums, 8 EPs, 3 live albums, 2 compilation albums, 12 singles, and 11 music videos. The band gained prominence with their debut album *Don't Turn Away* (1992) and achieved further success with *Big Choice* (1995), featuring the hit "Disconnected." After releasing several albums and undergoing lineup changes, the band disbanded in 2004 but reunited in 2008. They have since continued to perform and release new music, including their 2011 comeback album *Laugh Now, Laugh Later.*

The State Security Service (SSS) of Azerbaijan (Azerbaijani: *Dövlət Təhlükəsizlik Xidməti*) is a government agency formed on December 14, 2015, succeeding the Ministry of National Security. Ali Naghiyev is the current agency executive. The SSS operates based on the Constitution, national laws, and international agreements, with a focus on human rights. The agency also has an academy, medical center, and cultural center.

The Mississippi Wing Civil Air Patrol is the state-level branch of the Civil Air Patrol in Mississippi. With over 500 members, it supports emergency services, cadet programs, and aerospace education. The wing conducts search and rescue missions, disaster relief, and provides training for youth and the public.

Ben Christo (born March 22, 1980) is an English guitarist, vocalist, and songwriter, best known as the lead guitarist of The Sisters of Mercy since 2006. He has also fronted bands AKO, Night by Night, and Diamond Black, and has worked as a session musician for artists including Lord of the Lost and PIG.

*Bobby* is a 2002 Indian Telugu-language romantic drama film directed and written by Sobhan. Starring Mahesh Babu and Aarthi Agarwal, the film follows the love story of Bobby, the son of a wealthy businessman, and Bhagyamati, the daughter of a mafia leader, whose fathers are arch rivals. Despite their families' enmity, the couple's love leads to reconciliation. Produced by K. Krishna Mohan Rao with music by Mani Sharma, the film received mixed reviews for its lack of originality.

Ruth Ella Moore (May 19, 1903 – July 19, 1994) was an American bacteriologist, microbiologist, and fashion designer. She is recognized as the first African-American woman to earn a Ph.D. in the natural sciences, receiving her doctorate in bacteriology from Ohio State University in 1933. Her groundbreaking research included studies on tuberculosis, blood types, dental caries, and gut microbiomes, contributing significantly to public health and microbiology. Moore was also the first woman to chair a department at Howard University, where she led the Department of Bacteriology (later renamed Microbiology) and taught for over three decades.

In addition to her scientific achievements, Moore was a talented seamstress and fashion designer, creating elegant garments that reflected her passion for style. She received numerous honors during her lifetime, including the Centennial Award for Distinguished Alumni from Ohio State University. Moore passed away in Rockville, Maryland, in 1994, leaving a legacy of scientific and creative excellence.

The 2018 New Hampshire elections were held on November 6, 2018, with primary elections on June 5. Democrats gained control of the New Hampshire Executive Council, State Senate, and State House of Representatives. Both U.S. House seats were retained by Democrats, while Republican Governor Chris Sununu was reelected. No U.S. Senate election was held in the state.
Li Shuai (born August 18, 1982) is a Chinese former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He played for Qingdao Jonoon, Guangzhou Evergrande, and Shanghai Shenhua.

Dingli (Maltese: Ħad-Dingli) is a village in the Northern Region of Malta, with a population of 3,711 as of January 2019. It is located on a plateau, approximately 250 metres above sea level, one of the highest points of Malta. The village overlooks the open sea from the Dingli Cliffs, located about a kilometre from the village.

The name "Dingli" is believed to be derived from Sir Thomas Dingley, an English knight. Rock-cut tombs dating back to Phoenician, Carthaginian and Roman times have been found in the area. Until the 16th century, a village called Ħal Tartarni existed near Dingli. The parish of Dingli was established in 1678.

The Taguig Integrated Terminal Exchange (TITX) is a planned intermodal transport terminal in Arca South, Taguig, Philippines. Owned by Arca South Integrated Terminal, Inc., a subsidiary of Ayala Land, it was awarded as a public-private partnership project in 2015. Construction began in 2018 after delays, with an estimated cost of ₱5.20 billion. The terminal, expected to serve 160,000 passengers daily, was postponed due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and its opening remains pending.

The Committee of the Verkhovna Rada on Environmental Policy, Nature Resources Utilization, and Elimination of the Consequences of the Chornobyl Catastrophe is a parliamentary committee in Ukraine overseeing environmental policy, natural resource management, and Chornobyl disaster recovery. Established in 1990, it plays a key role in shaping Ukraine's ecological legislation and international environmental agreements. The committee consists of 15 deputies and operates under the Verkhovna Rada. Its chairmanship has been vacant since March 2016.

Robert Gilbert Haney Jr. (1926–2004) was an American comic book writer, best known for his work for DC Comics. He co-created the Teen Titans, Metamorpho, and the Super-Sons. Haney also wrote for animated television shows.

Tim Greene (born 1969) is a South African actor, film director, and writer, best known for his debut feature film *Boy Called Twist*. The film pioneered innovative crowdfunding in South African cinema and premiered in 2004.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer and manager, known for his versatility as a half-back. Born in Beamish, County Durham, he played for several clubs, including Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday, and Macclesfield, where he captained the team to a league championship. Burridge later served as player-manager at Hurst, making over 100 appearances before his death in 1977.

Sina Karimian (born January 23, 1988) is an Iranian professional kickboxer and the inaugural K-1 Cruiserweight champion. Competing since 2018, he has achieved notable victories, including multiple title defenses and wins against prominent fighters such as Hisaki Kato and Kyotaro Fujimoto. Known for his orthodox stance and fighting out of Iran, Karimian has a professional record of 14 wins, including 7 by knockout, and 2 losses.

Milltown, County Kildare (Irish: Baile an Mhuilinn) is a village in Ireland located in the province of Leinster. As of the 2016 Census, it has a population of 344. The village features the Church of St. Brigid, built in 1817, and is home to the Milltown GAA sports club, established in 1888.

Bygdøy Royal Estate is a Baroque estate in Oslo, Norway, owned by the Norwegian royal family. Originally belonging to a Cistercian monastery, it was acquired by the king during the Protestant Reformation. The current main building was erected in 1733. The estate is now the regular summer residence of Harald V and Queen Sonja.

Egon Albrecht-Lemke (19 May 1918 – 25 August 1944) was a German Luftwaffe fighter pilot during World War II. Credited with 25 aerial victories, including six four-engine bombers, he commanded fighter units on both the Eastern and Western Fronts. He was awarded the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross before being killed in action by USAAF fighters in 1944.

Jawadia Arabic College, established in 1928 in Banaras, Uttar Pradesh, India, by Maulana Syed Mohammad Sajjad Al-Husaini, is a prominent institution for higher Islamic studies, focusing on Shia jurisprudence, theology, and literature.

Oxhorn Run is a 1.8-mile-long (2.9 km) tributary of Painter Run in Davidson Township, Sullivan County, Pennsylvania. Originating on the edge of a plateau, it flows through a deepening valley before joining Painter Run. The stream's watershed spans 0.87 square miles (2.3 km²) and lies within the Upper Susquehanna-Lackawanna drainage basin. Oxhorn Run is impaired due to atmospheric deposition and pH issues, making it the only non-attaining stream in the Painter Run watershed.

Greg Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Arkansas Senate from the 16th district from 2015 until his death in 2017. A graduate of Arkansas Tech University, he won a special election to succeed Michael Lamoureux and passed away from cancer at age 50.

Nathan Sawaya (born July 10, 1973) is an American artist known for creating large-scale sculptures and installations using LEGO bricks. A former attorney, he gained recognition in 2004 after leaving his legal career to pursue art full-time. Sawaya's work, including his signature human form sculptures and large-scale replicas, has been exhibited globally in venues such as the Lancaster Museum of Art, Federation Square in Melbourne, and the ArtScience Museum in Singapore. His touring exhibition, *The Art of the Brick*, is one of the first major museum exhibitions to focus exclusively on LEGO as an art medium. Sawaya operates studios in Manhattan and Los Angeles, each housing over 1.5 million LEGO bricks, and has been recognized as one of the world's top LEGO builders. His work has been widely acclaimed, featured in major media outlets, and commissioned by collectors, celebrities, and institutions worldwide.

Patrick Kwok-Choon (born November 19, 1988) is a Canadian actor and director. He is known for his roles as Seth Park in *Open Heart* and Gen Rhys in *Star Trek: Discovery*. Kwok-Choon has also performed in numerous theatrical productions, including *War Horse* and *Kim's Convenience*.

Bert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer who played as a half-back. He began his career in non-league football before joining Darlington in 1921, helping them win promotion to the Second Division in 1925. He later played for Sheffield Wednesday, winning two First Division titles, and Oldham Athletic. After retiring from league football, he captained Macclesfield to two Cheshire County League titles and later became player-manager of Hurst.

Miniclip SA is a Swiss online game website and subsidiary launched in 2001. It is known for hosting a large collection of browser games, particularly Flash games. Miniclip has also developed and published mobile games for various platforms, including iOS, Android, and Windows Phone. Some of their popular mobile titles include 8 Ball Pool, Golf Battle, Gravity Guy, and Agar.io.

In 2012, Miniclip began distributing games on the Xbox division of Windows 8, with titles like Gravity Guy, iStunt 2, and Monster Island. In 2017, they released their first game compatible with Xbox One, PC, and PlayStation 4, titled MX Nitro. Miniclip was acquired by Tencent in 2015. The company has faced some security concerns, with reports of malicious software being distributed through downloads associated with the site in the mid-2000s.

AFSCME Council 31 is a labor union representing public employees in Illinois, formed from a group established during the Great Depression to protect jobs and public services. It became affiliated with the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME) in 1936. The union has played a significant role in advocating for collective bargaining rights and has been involved in various political actions and legal disputes, particularly during budget crises.

Carmen Contreras-Bozak (1919–2017) was the first Hispanic woman to serve in the Women's Army Corps (WAAC) during World War II. She transmitted encoded messages at General Eisenhower's headquarters in Algiers.

Howard Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American right-handed pitcher in Major League Baseball from 1915 to 1930. He played for the Buffalo Blues, Detroit Tigers, Boston Red Sox, and Philadelphia Athletics.

Ehmke is best known for his 1923 no-hitter against the Athletics while with the Red Sox, and for his unexpected Game 1 start in the 1929 World Series for the Athletics, in which he struck out a then-record 13 batters. He won a World Series title that year.

Prior to playing in the major leagues, Ehmke played in the Pacific Coast League for the Los Angeles Angels and in the New York State League for the Syracuse Stars. After his baseball career, Ehmke founded the Howard Ehmke Company, manufacturing tarpaulins, tents, flags, and banners. The company is still in operation today as Ehmke Manufacturing Company.

Gold Hill is an unincorporated community in Lee County, Alabama, United States. Settled in the 1830s, it was named after an early settler, Goldsmith, rather than the gold mined nearby during the Alabama Gold Rush. The community, also known as Gold Ridge in the 1870s, is home to historical landmarks, including the now-demolished 140-year-old barn.

Bertrand Ndongo is a Spanish political figure and consultant for the far-right party Vox, known for his controversial views on immigration and gender issues. Raised in a challenging family environment, he moved to Spain in 2010 and worked his way up from a cleaner to a management position. Ndongo has garnered attention for his provocative statements on social media and his criticism of both Vox and Spanish royalty.

"Hold On Me" is a song by Australian post-grunge band Grinspoon, released on February 21, 2005, as a single from their album *Thrills, Kills & Sunday Pills*. The song was written by Pat Davern and Phil Jamieson, and produced by Howard Benson.

The Australian Music Guide ranked "Hold On Me" as the 35th most-played song by an Australian artist in 2005. A FasterLouder magazine review in March 2005 described the song as "an inoffensive, nice song" with "catchy hooks". The review praised Jamieson's vocals on the chorus, while noting a certain "cheesiness" in some of the lyrics.

The music video for "Hold On Me" begins with Phil Jamieson briefly explaining the song's origins. The video, shot primarily in black and white, is styled to resemble a 1960s television performance.

La Grosse Décharge Est is a river located in the Capitale-Nationale region of Quebec, Canada, specifically within the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality and the city of Baie-Saint-Paul. The river originates from a small lake at an elevation of 673 meters (2,208 feet) and flows for 6.5 kilometers (4.0 miles) before emptying into the Rivière des Mares, a tributary of the Gouffre River, at an elevation of 380 meters (1,250 feet). The river descends 249 meters (817 feet) along its course, which is characterized by several streams on both sides. The name "La Grosse Décharge Est" was officially recognized on March 25, 1997, by the Commission de toponymie du Québec.

Iris variegata, commonly known as the Hungarian iris, is a species of flowering plant in the family Iridaceae. Native to Central and Eastern Europe, it is characterized by its yellow and purple-veined flowers. A diploid species (2n=24), it has been widely used in hybridization, particularly with *Iris pallida*, to produce garden varieties of tall bearded irises. It thrives in well-drained soils and sunny habitats, often found in open woodlands and steppes. Protected in some regions, it is cultivated for its ornamental value and hardiness.

*Riffs* is a live album by alto saxophonist Jimmy Lyons, released in 1982. It was recorded at Le Dreher club in Paris on September 13–14, 1980, and features Lyons with bassoonist Karen Borca, bassist Jay Oliver, and drummer Paul Murphy. The album contains lengthy versions of "Theme" and "Riffs #1/II".

A sponge city is an urban development concept adopted by the People's Republic of China to address the challenges of urban flooding and stormwater management. The initiative emerged in response to the inadequacies of traditional grey infrastructure, particularly highlighted by the devastating Beijing flood in 2012, which resulted in significant loss of life. The sponge city model emphasizes the natural retention, infiltration, and purification of rainwater, aiming for cities to harvest and reuse 70% of rainwater by 2030. 

The approach integrates green spaces, constructed wetlands, and other natural features to enhance urban water management, contrasting sharply with conventional methods that rely on centralized drainage systems. Despite its potential, the implementation of sponge cities faces challenges, including funding constraints and resistance from established urban planning practices. Pilot projects began in 2015, with cities like Zhenjiang and Xiamen leading the way. The sponge city concept has gained international interest, with applications in various climate zones around the world, as cities seek sustainable solutions to water management issues exacerbated by rapid urbanization.

Sir Daril Watson (17 October 1888 – 1 July 1967) was a British Army general who served in both World Wars. He commanded the 2nd Infantry Division, became Assistant Chief of the Imperial General Staff, and later led Western Command. He retired in 1947 as Quartermaster-General to the Forces.
"Radar's Report" is the third episode of the second season of the American television series *M*A*S*H*.

Samira Ćosović (Serbian Cyrillic: Самира Ћосовић) is a Serbian politician and teacher. A member of the Sandžak Democratic Party (SDPS), she has served in the Prijepolje municipal assembly since 2008 and was elected to the National Assembly in 2020. She later became deputy mayor of Prijepolje in 2021, resigning from parliament. Previously, she worked as a Serbian language teacher in Brodarevo.

Anna Marie Tendler (born June 9, 1985) is an American artist, writer, designer, and former makeup artist. She gained prominence as an early beauty influencer through her Tumblr blog and has worked on various television and Broadway productions. Tendler owns Silk Parlor, a business specializing in handcrafted Victorian-style lampshades, and has exhibited her artwork. She was married to comedian John Mulaney from 2014 to 2022.

Bolesław Szymański (born April 22, 1950) is a Polish-American computer scientist and Claire and Roland Schmitt Distinguished Professor at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute. Known for his work in parallel and distributed computing, he directs the Social Cognitive Networks Academic Research Center and has contributed to projects like MilkyWay@home. He is an IEEE Fellow and a member of the Polish Academy of Sciences.

Hassan Haskins (born November 26, 1999) is an American football running back for the Tennessee Titans of the NFL. A standout at Michigan, he set school records in 2021, including 20 rushing touchdowns in a single season.

Peter George Baines is an Australian researcher, academic, and author specializing in geophysical fluid dynamics, climate dynamics, oceanography, and volcano dynamics. He earned degrees in mathematics and physics from Melbourne University and a PhD in geophysical fluid dynamics from Cambridge University. Baines has held academic and research positions at institutions including the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, CSIRO Atmospheric Research, and the University of Melbourne, where he is an Honorary Professorial Fellow.

Baines' research has advanced understanding of stratified fluid flows, ocean tides, climate variability, and volcanic eruptions. He authored the influential book *Topographic Effects in Stratified Flows* and has received numerous accolades, including the AMOS Priestley Medal. He has also contributed to international meteorological organizations and served on editorial boards, making significant contributions to atmospheric and oceanic sciences.
Bharatpur is an assembly constituency in Murshidabad district in the Indian state of West Bengal.

Millennium Fantasy is a two-movement orchestral composition by Ellen Taaffe Zwilich, lasting approximately 20 minutes. Scored for solo piano and orchestra, it incorporates the folk song *"Come All You Fair and Tender Ladies,"* a melody from the composer's childhood. Premiered to positive reception, critics praised its dialogue between piano and orchestra and its enduring appeal. A recording featuring pianist Jeffrey Biegel and the Florida State University Symphony Orchestra was released by Naxos Records in 2010.

Dancing with the Stars (American TV series) season 14 was the fourteenth season of the American dance competition series, airing on ABC from March 19 to May 22, 2012. Hosted by Tom Bergeron and Brooke Burke Charvet, the season featured twelve celebrity contestants paired with professional dancers, judged by Carrie Ann Inaba, Len Goodman, and Bruno Tonioli. The season introduced new professional dancers Henry Byalikov and Emma Slater to the troupe.  

The season included themed weeks such as Rock Week, Latin Week, Motown Week, and Classical Week, with eliminations occurring weekly. Donald Driver, an NFL wide receiver, and his partner Peta Murgatroyd won the competition, marking Murgatroyd's first victory as a professional. The season also featured a Trio Week, where remaining couples performed with an additional eliminated pro or troupe member. Music was provided by the Harold Wheeler orchestra throughout the season.

"The Holy Science" is a book by Sri Yukteswar that compares Sanskrit slokas with passages from the New Testament, particularly the Book of Revelation. It is divided into four sections reflecting stages of knowledge development and introduces Yukteswar's Yuga theory, positing that the current age is Dwapara Yuga, beginning around 1699 A.D.

Stephen Lake (born December 1963) is a British Anglican bishop and former Dean of Gloucester, currently serving as the Bishop of Salisbury. Born in Poole, Dorset, he was ordained in 1988 and held various roles, including vicar, Rural Dean of Poole, and Sub-Dean of St Albans Cathedral. As Dean of Gloucester (2011–2022), he led significant projects like Project Pilgrim and chaired city regeneration initiatives. Consecrated as bishop in April 2022, Lake is also a long-time Scout Association member and Tottenham Hotspur fan.

One Nation Labour is a political ideology within the UK Labour Party, primarily associated with former leader Ed Miliband. It advocates for an economy that addresses regional disparities, particularly in northern Britain, and aims to support low- and middle-income earners through long-term investment and a focus on manufacturing. The movement emphasizes the importance of technical education for the "forgotten 50 percent," arguing that the nation's economic future relies on individuals with mid-level skills.

One Nation Labour also champions social justice, calling for equal life chances and accountability across all societal levels, particularly in addressing income inequality and corporate responsibility. It promotes a vision of British identity rooted in openness and tolerance while advocating for immigration policies that prioritize the economic interests of the working class.

Additionally, One Nation Labour supports the devolution of powers to local governments in England, mirroring the devolution experienced by Scotland and Wales. Ideologically, it challenges aspects of the Third Way policies of New Labour, embracing a more public acknowledgment of socialism while advocating for a capitalism that serves the people and fosters a fairer, more equitable society.

Judith Arnold, born Barbara Keiler on April 7, 1953, is an American novelist known for her extensive work in the romance genre, having published over 85 novels since 1983. A graduate of Smith College and Brown University, she has received multiple awards for her writing and was involved in a notable class action suit against Harlequin Enterprises regarding e-book royalties.

Tracy Reiner (born July 7, 1964) is an American actress known for roles in *Die Hard* (1988), *When Harry Met Sally...* (1989), and *A League of Their Own* (1992). She is the daughter of Penny Marshall and was adopted by Rob Reiner.

Dmitry Tikhonovich Lisanevich (1778 – July 1825) was a Russian general known for his military service during the Persian Expedition, Russo-Persian War, and Caucasian War. He gained recognition for his leadership in key battles, including the capture of Ganja and the conquest of Imeretia. Lisanevich was awarded several honors, including the Order of St. George and the Order of Saint Vladimir. He died from wounds sustained in combat while attempting to quell uprisings in the Caucasus.

Nutrisystem is an American company specializing in weight loss and weight management products and services. Founded in 1972 by Harold Katz, the company is headquartered in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania, and operates in the United States and Canada. Nutrisystem offers pre-packaged meals, nutrition plans, and counseling through telephone, internet, and mobile platforms. It is a subsidiary of Kainos Capital, with Stephen Mikulak serving as its president.

A 2015 systematic review found that Nutrisystem showed promise for short-term weight loss compared to control groups, though no studies on its long-term efficacy were available. The program's cost, averaging $274.99 for a four-week basic plan as of 2019, has been criticized as potentially prohibitive. Additionally, experts note that the diet's structured nature may complicate social eating. Despite these criticisms, Nutrisystem remains a prominent option for individuals seeking structured weight management solutions.
Louis of Brienne (fl. 13th century) was a French nobleman, son of John of Brienne, and Viscount of Beaumont through marriage.

The Acheloos Painter was an ancient Greek vase painter active in Athens around 525–500 BCE, associated with the black-figure style and the Leagros Group. He is named after his "name vase," a black-figure amphora depicting Heracles battling the river god Acheloos, portrayed as a horned centaur. Known for his comic and detailed depictions, his works often juxtaposed heroic mythology with humorous or exaggerated elements. His notable pieces include scenes of mythological battles and everyday life.

An acoustic torpedo is a self-guided underwater weapon that uses sonar technology to detect and track targets, such as submarines or ships. Equipped with acoustic transducers, these torpedoes can operate in passive mode, detecting noise from the target's machinery or propellers, or in active mode, emitting sonar pulses to locate the target. Once launched, they function as "fire-and-forget" weapons, capable of homing in on targets even during evasive maneuvers or in the presence of countermeasures.

First deployed during World War II, acoustic torpedoes had a significant impact on naval warfare, particularly in the Battle of the Atlantic. German U-boats used the T5 torpedo to target Allied ships, but the Allies countered with innovations like the Foxer noisemaker and tactical maneuvers such as "Step-Aside." These countermeasures significantly reduced the effectiveness of German torpedoes.

Postwar developments included advanced decoy systems like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie, used by modern navies to defend against acoustic torpedoes. Captured German technology, such as the T5 torpedo, also contributed to advancements in Allied and Soviet naval weaponry. Acoustic torpedoes remain a critical component of naval defense and offense, evolving with advancements in sonar and guidance systems.

The Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) is a semi-aquatic mammal belonging to the family Mustelidae, characterized by its brown fur, cream underbelly, and streamlined body adapted for swimming. This species is widely distributed across Europe, parts of Asia, and North Africa, inhabiting clean freshwater bodies and coastal areas. The Eurasian otter primarily feeds on fish but also consumes amphibians and small mammals. Classified as Near Threatened by the IUCN, its populations have been recovering in many regions due to conservation efforts, despite historical declines caused by pollution and habitat loss.

Karl Hermann Heinrich Benda (1748–1836) was a German violinist, pianist, and composer, the son of Franz Benda. A member of the Berlin court orchestra, he was a renowned violin virtuoso and teacher to Prussian royalty.
Tasneem Roc (born 11 December 1978) is an Australian actress known for her roles in *East West 101*, *Reef Doctors*, and *Kath & Kimderella*.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is a musical piece composed by Nino Rota, originally featured in the 1972 film "The Godfather." The song has been adapted into various languages, with lyrics by Larry Kusik, and has seen multiple recordings, including versions by Roger Williams and Andy Williams. Rota's score for the film was nominated for an Academy Award, although it was disqualified due to prior use in another film.

Hopanoids are a diverse class of pentacyclic triterpenoids produced by many bacteria. Analogous to sterols in eukaryotes, hopanoids modulate the fluidity and permeability of bacterial cell membranes, aiding adaptation to extreme environments. They are synthesized from squalene via squalene-hopene cyclase, and are often further modified with functional groups. These modifications influence their packing within the membrane and their specific functions, such as stress tolerance and nitrogen fixation.

Hopanoids are among the most abundant natural products on Earth and are preserved in sediments over geological timescales as hopanes, making them valuable biomarkers for studying the evolution of life. While 2-methylhopanes were initially proposed as biomarkers for oxygenic photosynthesis by cyanobacteria, this has been disputed, with research suggesting other bacterial sources. Similarly, 3-methylhopanoids were thought to be markers of aerobic methanotrophy, but are now known to be produced by a wider range of bacteria. Hopanoids have potential applications in industry, agriculture as biofertilizers, and medicine by influencing multidrug transport.

Yankee Pasha is a 1954 American adventure film directed by Joseph Pevney, based on the novel by Edison Marshall. Starring Jeff Chandler, Rhonda Fleming, and Mamie Van Doren, the film follows fur trapper Jason Starbuck as he pursues his love, Roxana, who is captured by pirates and sold into slavery in Morocco.

TVR Cultural is a Romanian television channel owned by Televiziunea Română. Originally launched in 2002, it ceased broadcasting in 2012 due to financial difficulties, but relaunched in 2022 in both SD and HD.

The American Federation of Aviculture (AFA) is an educational organization founded in 1974 to represent bird clubs and protect aviculture. Based in Austin, Texas, the AFA's mission is to promote aviculture through education, improved breeding and husbandry practices, conservation, research, and legislative awareness.

The AFA hosts an annual convention featuring speakers on aviculture topics. It publishes the AFA Watchbird magazine and other publications, and offers a two-level certification program called Fundamentals of Aviculture. The AFA also distributes grants for research and wild parrot conservation projects. Its legislative committee monitors legislation that may impact the keeping and breeding of companion parrots. The organization has eleven geographically segmented regions of members.
Sophie Cook is a British transgender activist, journalist, and former RAF technician.

April Carrión (born April 23, 1989) is a Puerto Rican drag performer and artist, best known as a contestant on *RuPaul's Drag Race* (season 6). They have appeared in music videos, documentaries, and television shows, including *Mala Mala* and *Versus Drag Queens*.
Kleiner Tiergarten is a historic urban park in Moabit, Berlin.

*Two from Galilee* (1972) is a Christian novel by Marjorie Holmes that retells the biblical story of Mary and Joseph, from their betrothal to the birth of Jesus. Despite mixed critical reception, it became a bestseller and inspired a musical adaptation.
The Antifascist Committee of Ukraine is an anti-fascist and pro-Russian organization, closely linked to the Communist Party of Ukraine.

In mathematics, particularly topology, one describes a manifold using an atlas. An atlas consists of individual charts that, roughly speaking, describe individual regions of the manifold. If the manifold is the surface of the Earth, then an atlas has its more common meaning. In general, the notion of atlas underlies the formal definition of a manifold and related structures such as vector bundles and other fiber bundles.

The Beach 90th Street station (signed as Beach 90th Street–Holland station) is a station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway. It is served by the Rockaway Park Shuttle at all times and ten daily rush-hour only A trains.

The National Audit Office (NAO) of Tanzania is an independent Parliamentary body in Tanzania which is responsible for auditing central government departments, government agencies and non-departmental public bodies. The NAO also carries out value for money (VFM) audits into the administration of public policy. The office is headed by the Chief Auditor General appointed by the President of Tanzania.

The Pulmonary Vascular Research Institute (PVRI) is a UK registered medical research charity. It is registered Charity in the United Kingdom (Charity No: 1127115) and a private limited company by guarantee. The company registration number in the United Kingdom is 5780068. The PVRI is a professional membership organisation for doctors and scientists from around the world who have a special interest in pulmonary hypertension (PH) or pulmonary vascular diseases (PVD).
The Museum of Musical Instruments of Céret (French: Musée des instruments de Céret), also known as MúSIC, is located in Céret (Pyrénées-Orientales).

Basugaon (IPA:ˈbɑːsʊˌgɑ̃ʊ) is a Town in Chirang District of Assam, India. The name of this town is derived from its high bamboo plantation and bamboo market basically "basu" or "bash" means bamboo and "gaon" means village that is Basugaon, 'The village of bamboo'. It is an industrial town and a Municipal Board area. It is a neighbourhood of Bongaigaon UA, and it is situated approximately 180 km from Guwahati City in the western part of the Lower Assam Region.
Alexandra Pringle, Hon. FRSL (born 1952/53), is a British publisher. She was a founding director of Virago Press and the editor-in-chief of Bloomsbury Publishing for 18 years as of March 2018.

The Dornier Do 11 was a German heavy bomber, developed in secret in the early 1930s. It was originally called the Dornier F before being renamed by the Reichsluftfahrtministerium (RLM) in 1933, and was considered a heavy bomber at the time. It came into service in 1932, a continuation of a line of bomber designs from the Dornier Do P in 1930, and the Dornier Do Y in 1931. The line would continue to develop with the Dornier Do 13 and Dornier Do 23.

Chenani is a town and tehsil in the Udhampur district, in the Indian union territory of Jammu and Kashmir. It is governed by a notified area council (city council). Before 1947, it was an internal jagir ruled by a Chandel (Rajput) ruler in the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir.

Furcifer tuzetae is a species of chameleon that is endemic to Madagascar. It is only known from its type locality, Andrenalamivola near Befandriana Sud. It was described by Édouard-Raoul Brygoo, Robert M. Bourgat and Charles Antoine Domergue in 1972. The International Union for Conservation of Nature have rated this species as "data deficient".
Jamil Baroody (1905 – March 4, 1979) was a Saudi diplomat who served as a representative to the United Nations from the organization's first meeting in 1946 until his death.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – 1784), also known as Theodorick Bland, Sr. or Theodorick Bland of Cawsons, was Virginia planter who served as a member of the first Virginia Senate, as well as a militia officer and clerk of Prince George County, Virginia.

Sir Edward Aloysius McTiernan, KBE (16 February 1892 – 9 January 1990), was an Australian lawyer, politician, and judge. He served on the High Court of Australia from 1930 to 1976, the longest-serving judge in the court's history.

McTiernan was born in Glen Innes, New South Wales. He graduated from the University of Sydney in 1915, and was called to the bar the following year. McTiernan was elected to the New South Wales Legislative Assembly in 1920, representing the Labor Party, and was soon after appointed Attorney-General of New South Wales. He served as attorney-general under John Storey, James Dooley, and Jack Lang, but left state politics in 1927. McTiernan was elected to the House of Representatives in 1929, but served for little over a year before Prime Minister James Scullin nominated him to the High Court. He was 38 at the time; only H. V. Evatt (another Scullin nominee) was appointed at a younger age. On the court, McTiernan was considered a moderate, and was known for the consistency of his decisions. He generally favoured the position of the federal government, upholding the constitutionality of contentious legislation from both sides of politics. McTiernan retired reluctantly at the age of 84, after just under 46 years on the High Court bench. He lived to the age of 97, and was the last surviving MP from the 1920s.

Peredur (IPA-cy, Old Welsh Peretur) is the name of a number of men from the boundaries of history and legend in sub-Roman Britain. The Peredur who is most familiar to a modern audience is the character who made his entrance as a knight in the Arthurian world of Middle Welsh prose literature.

The New York World was a newspaper published in New York City from 1860 until 1931. The paper played a major role in the history of American newspapers. It was a leading national voice of the Democratic Party. From 1883 to 1911 under publisher Joseph Pulitzer, it was a pioneer in yellow journalism, capturing readers' attention with sensation, sports, sex and scandal and pushing its daily circulation to the one-million mark. It was sold in 1930 and merged into the New York World-Telegram.

Antasena-class combat boat is a combat boat built by North Sea Boats and designed by LOMOcean Design for the Indonesian armed forces. The manufacturer designated it as Combat Boat X-18 / CB X-18. Initially, this class of ships was developed from a tank boat program from a consortium consisting of PT Lundin (North Sea Boats), PT Pindad, PT LEN, and PT Hariff Daya Tunggal Engineering, with John Cockerill as the turret supplier.

Trochilinae is one of the six subfamilies that make up the hummingbird family Trochilidae.

The subfamily is divided into three tribes: Lampornithini (mountain gems) containing 18 species, Mellisugini (bees) containing 37 species and Trochilini (emeralds) containing 115 species.

The Redan Football Netball Club (nicknamed the Lions) is an Australian rules football and netball club located in the city of Ballarat, Victoria. The football team currently competes in the Ballarat Football League. Redan has a history of 59 Premierships including 11 Senior Football Premierships and was named the Victorian Country Football League Most Disciplined Club in 2004 & 2005.

Redan players have also won the Henderson Medal (Ballarat Football League's best player award) on 14 occasions with two players, Michael Smith (1994, 1994) and Jarrod Edwards (2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009) winning the award in consecutive years. Edwards is the first player in BFL history to win four consecutive Henderson Medals.

The club also has four winners of the Tony Lockett Medal, for the leading goalscorer in the Ballarat Football League. Redan currently play their home matches at City Oval in Ballarat and play in a predominantly maroon strip with a maroon top band and a lion logo on the chest.

The first Redan game was reported in The Ballarat Star Saturday September 2, 1871 against the Ballarat Football Club. The Redan team was made up from miners from the Band of Hope mine and their average weight was 14 Stone (90 kg). The Star reported that the Redan team were everywhere, kicking and tripping regardless of the consequences.

Former Adelaide Crows coach, the late Phil Walsh, played for Redan prior to be being recruited by Collingwood.

Might and Magic: Heroes Kingdoms (originally Heroes of Might and Magic Kingdoms) was a real-time strategy massively multiplayer online game based on the Heroes of Might and Magic series. The game was announced in 2007 with closed betas running until 2009. It was released in France on 3 November 2009. It closed its servers on 1 September 2014.

Amanda Jaqueline Paswall (née DaCosta; born 7 October 1989) is an American-born Portuguese former professional footballer who played as a midfielder for Washington Spirit, Chicago Red Stars and Boston Breakers in the National Women's Soccer League (NWSL), Liverpool Ladies in England's FA WSL, the Washington Freedom and MagicJack in the Women's Professional Soccer (WPS) and for the Portuguese national team.

Ambient techno is a subgenre of techno that incorporates the atmospheric textures of ambient music with the rhythmic elements and production of techno. It was pioneered by 1990s electronic artists such as Aphex Twin, Carl Craig, the Black Dog, Pete Namlook and Biosphere.
Animal Control Products Limited is a state-owned enterprise of the New Zealand government. Its main business is the manufacture and sale of pest management products.

Kihikihi, a small town located in the Waikato region of the North Island of New Zealand, serves as a satellite community of Te Awamutu, five kilometres to the north, and lies 35 kilometres south of Hamilton. The 2018 New Zealand census recorded a population of 2,808 people The main reason for the large increase since 2013 is the construction of a large number of new dwellings.

The town's outer rim has merged with the expanding rim of Te Awamutu, rendering the boundary between the two towns difficult to perceive.

Kihikihi is a Māori-language word meaning "cicada"; the name imitates the sound made by the insect. A large statue of a cicada stands at the northern entrance to the town.

Kihikihi's multi-purpose sports domain hosts national and international equestrian events such as the FEI Eventing World Cup.

The town is also home to the historic Kihikihi Polo Club, founded in 1892 by the Kay family.

Malice Mizer (stylized as MALICE MIZER) was a Japanese visual kei rock band active from August 1992 to December 2001. Formed by guitarists Mana and Közi, their earlier music and themes were characterized by their strong French and classical influences, later moving away from deliberate French romanticism and incorporating Gothic aspects after several difficulties befell the band. Their official fan club is named "Ma chérie".

Malice Mizer was as famous for their live shows as they were for their music, featuring lavish historical costumes and stage sets, short silent theater pieces preluding various songs, and even a particularly notable instance of the vocalist descending to Earth (the stage) as a fallen angel, only to ascend again at the end of the concert. Throughout their history, the band has gone through several different lineups and three drastic image changes. At their peak, they were considered one of "the big four of visual kei" alongside La'cryma Christi, Fanatic Crisis and Shazna. On December 11, 2001, it was announced that Malice Mizer would go on an indefinite hiatus. Mana, Közi and bassist Yu~ki have performed together several times since 2010.

Worms Blast is a puzzle video game for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 2, GameCube, Game Boy Advance and Mac OS X released in 2002, developed by Team17, and published by Ubi Soft. The Mac version was developed and published by Feral Interactive.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (also known as the Gershman Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (GPJFF)) is an annual film festival in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, in the United States that presents movies and film-related programs about the Jewish experience, culture, values, and legacy.

"Ghost Town" is a song by American rock band Cheap Trick, released in 1988 as the third single from their tenth studio album Lap of Luxury. It was written by Diane Warren and guitarist Rick Nielsen, and produced by Richie Zito. The song reached number 33 on the Billboard Hot 100.

"Ghost Town" dated back to 1981 when Nielsen recorded a demo of the song during sessions for the band's One on One album. The song remained undeveloped until the sessions for Lap of Luxury when Warren added some contributions to the finished song.
George Henson Estes Jr. (January 30, 1873 – July 2, 1969) was a United States Army officer in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

FC Emmen is a Dutch football club based in Emmen, Drenthe. They play in the Eredivisie, the top tier of Dutch football, after winning the 2021–22 Eerste Divisie title. Founded in 1925, the club entered the professional Eerste Divisie in 1985. In 2018, Emmen were promoted to the Eredivisie for the first time, but in 2021 they were relegated to the Eerste Divisie again. 2022 they were promoted again and play the 2022-2023 season in the Eredivisie again. Home games are played at De Oude Meerdijk.

The roadometer was a 19th-century device like an odometer for measuring mileage, mounted on a wagon wheel. One such device was invented in 1847 by William Clayton, Orson Pratt, and Appleton Harmon, pioneers of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints.

Demetrios John "Mitch" Skandalakis is an American lawyer and former Republican Party politician from Georgia who rose quickly to national prominence in the early-1990s. He upset Martin Luther King III to become chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners and in 1998, was the unsuccessful Republican nominee for lieutenant governor of Georgia. Afterward, he became subjected to a wide-ranging federal corruption investigation of Fulton County government and admitted lying to an FBI agent. He was disbarred, but was later reinstated, as an attorney.

The Fairmont Century Plaza is a landmark 19-story luxury hotel in Los Angeles. Located in Century City, the hotel forms a sweeping crescent design fronting the Avenue of the Stars, adjacent to the twin Century Plaza Towers and the 2000 Avenue of the Stars complex. At the time of its opening in 1966, the Century Plaza Hotel was the highest building in Century City, with views extending all the way to the Pacific Ocean. It was also the first hotel to have color televisions in all of its rooms. The hotel closed for renovations in 2016, and reopened on September 27, 2021. It is now operated by Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.

The Bernina Railway (Lang-de; Lang-it; Lang-rm) is a single-track 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in) metre gauge railway line forming part of the Rhaetian Railway (RhB). It links the spa resort of St. Moritz, in the canton of Graubünden, Switzerland, with the town of Tirano, in the Province of Sondrio, Italy, via the Bernina Pass. Reaching a height of 2,253 metres (7,392 ft) above sea level, it is the highest railway crossing in Europe and the third-highest railway in Switzerland. It also ranks as the highest adhesion railway of the continent, and – with inclines of up to 7% – as one of the steepest adhesion railways in the world. The elevation difference on the section between the Bernina Pass and Tirano is 1,824 m (5,984 ft), allowing passengers to view glaciers along the line.

On 7 July 2008, the Bernina Railway and the Albula Railway, which also forms part of the RhB, were recorded in the list of UNESCO World Heritage Sites, under the name Rhaetian Railway in the Albula / Bernina Landscapes. The whole site is a cross-border joint Swiss-Italian heritage area.

Trains operating on the Bernina Railway include the Bernina Express.

Caserma Ederle (Camp Ederle) is a military complex in Vicenza, Italy, where the United States Army has troops stationed. It is under Italian military control and can be managed anytime by the Italian authorities. The Vicenza Military Community is composed of soldiers, family members, civilians and retirees with a small number of airmen and sailors also stationed there. The post serves as the headquarters of United States Army Africa and the 173rd Airborne Brigade. Caserma Ederle serves as the headquarters of U.S. Army Garrison Italy of the United States Army Installation Management Command, an umbrella for all U.S. military properties in Vicenza. The post is named after Major Carlo Ederle, an Italian hero of World War I and recipient of the French Croix de Guerre (French War Cross), among other military honors.
John Doyley (1602–1660) was an English politician who sat in the House of Commons from 1646 to 1648.
Sina Karimian (born 23 January 1988) is an Iranian kickboxer, fighting out of Power of Dream Gym. He is first and current K-1 WORLD GP Cruiserweight Champion.
Caesium bisulfate or cesium hydrogen sulfate is an inorganic compound with the formula CsHSO4. The caesium salt of bisulfate, it is a colorless solid obtained by combining Cs2SO4 and H2SO4.

High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is a type of tumour that arises from the serous epithelial layer in the abdominopelvic cavity and is mainly found in the ovary. HGSCs make up the majority of ovarian cancer cases and have the lowest survival rates. HGSC is distinct from low-grade serous carcinoma (LGSC) which arises from ovarian tissue, is less aggressive and is present in stage I ovarian cancer where tumours are localised to the ovary.

Although originally thought to arise from the squamous epithelial cell layer covering the ovary, HGSC is now thought to originate in the Fallopian tube epithelium. HGSC is much more invasive than LGSC with a higher fatality rate - although it is more sensitive to platinum-based chemotherapy, possibly due to its rapid growth rate. In rare cases, HGSCs can develop from LGSCs, but generally the two types arise independently of each other.

General elections were due to be held for the Legislative Council in Nyasaland on 28 April 1964, and would have been the first in the country under universal suffrage. However, there were no opposition candidates to either the Malawi Congress Party in the general roll seats (the Nyasaland Asian Convention had dissolved itself and declared its support for the MCP), or the Nyasaland Constitutional Party in the special roll seats, resulting in all 53 candidates winning without votes being cast.

MCP leader Hastings Banda remained as Prime Minister, leading the country to independence on 6 July that year.

Parasol (1800–1826) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse. In total she won twenty of her thirty-five races, including two Newmarket First October King's Plates, the Jockey-Club Plate and a match race against Derby winner Cardinal Beaufort. Her only race away from Newmarket was for her début, in the Oaks Stakes in 1803. She was bred and owned by Augustus FitzRoy, 3rd Duke of Grafton. As a broodmare she foaled the stallion Partisan, 2000 Guineas winner Pindarrie and Pastille, who won both the 2000 Guineas and the Oaks.

Annie Féolde is a French chef, best known for holding three Michelin stars at her restaurant Enoteca Pinchiorri in Florence, Italy. She was the first female chef in Italy to win that level of accolade, and only the fourth ever.
The Neradje Mosque or Neradjes (Lang-el) is a historical Ottoman-era mosque located in the old town of Rethymno, Crete, Greece. It now serves as a music school.

Ports North, the trading name of the Far North Queensland Ports Corporation Limited, is a Queensland Government statutory corporation that is responsible for the Cairns Marlin Marina and the Cairns Cityport project and the ports in Cairns, Cape Flattery, Karumba, Mourilyan, Skardon River, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Burketown and Cooktown, in Queensland, Australia. Since 2017, the shareholding Ministers are the Honourable Jackie Trad, Deputy Premier, Treasurer and Minister for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Partnerships, and the Honourable Mark Bailey, Minister for Transport and Main Roads.

Formerly known as the Cairns Port Authority, the corporation was responsible for the operation of the Cairns International Airport until the sale of the airport in December 2008 to a private consortium. The former authority was responsible for the Cairns Seaport and in 2009 became responsible for the regional ports of Mourilyan, Cooktown, Cape Flattery, Quintell Beach, Thursday Island, Skardon River, Karumba and Burketown.

Joel Spitzer (born c. 1957) is an American smoking cessation educator. He currently serves as technical advisor at WhyQuit.com, a free nicotine dependence recovery website. All of his videos and writings on WhyQuit.com end with his advice, "Never Take Another Puff!"
Michel Deguy (23 May 1930 – 16 February 2022) was a French poet and translator.

Potamia (Greek: Ποταμιά, Turkish: Potamya, Bodamya and Dereliköy) is a village in the north-east of Cyprus in the district of Nicosia, close to the Green Line separating it from the area of the breakaway, unrecognised Northern Cyprus. Together with Pyla and Rizokarpaso, Potamia forms one of the few remaining ethnically mixed communities in Cyprus, made up of Greek and Turkish Cypriots.
The Botswana National Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Gaborone, Botswana. It is currently used mostly for football matches. The stadium holds 25,000 people.

Franco-Albertans (Lang-fr) are francophone residents of the Canadian province of Alberta. Franco-Albertans may also refer to residents of Alberta with French Canadian ancestry, although publications from the government of Alberta use the term Franco-Albertan to refer to its francophone residents. In the 2016 Canadian Census, there were 86,705 Albertans that stated their mother tongue was French. In the same census, there were 268,615 Albertans that claimed to be able to speak French.

Francophones were the first Europeans to visit the province, with French Canadian voyageurs employed in the fur trade exploring the region in the late 18th century. French Canadians settled into a number of communities in the Northwest Territories during the 19th century, including communities in present day Alberta. Several French toponyms exist in Alberta, exemplifying the Francophone presence in the region. In 1928, the Association canadienne-française de l'Alberta was formed to promote francophone rights, and to lobby the interests of Franco-Albertans to the province. Following the enactment of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms in 1982, Franco-Albertans pushed for further linguistic rights within the province, eventually resulting in the province establishing an independent public francophone school system in 1994.

Approximately 72 per cent of Franco-Albertans are situated within the province's two largest cities, Edmonton, and Calgary. The province is home to more than 100 francophone non-profit organizations. Radio-Canada, the country's French-language public broadcaster, serves as the main French-language media broadcaster in Alberta.

My Mowgli Boy (Chinese: 我的莫格利男孩) is a 2019 Chinese urban romance comedy television series directed by Peng Xuejun. It stars Ma Tianyu as Mowgli and Yang Zi as Ling Xi in the lead roles. It premiered on iQIYI on August 29, 2019 and finished broadcasting on October 10, 2019. It also aired primetime on Shenzhen Satellite TV starting from April 12th 2020.

The series tells the story of Mo Ge Li, a boy raised in the jungle by the forest caretaker, who is accidentally brought into the city by Ling Xi, an independent entrepreneurial woman and called on people to protect the environment.
The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military liaison and observation aircraft produced by the Taylorcraft Aeroplanes (England) Limited company during the Second World War.

Burlington Street is a partially at grade and elevated roadway in Hamilton, Ontario, stretching along the south shore of Hamilton Harbour in Lake Ontario. Burlington Street has four different statuses along the route. In its western terminus, it is a collector route ending at Bay Street North. Upon the intersection at Wellington Street, the road becomes an arterial route with four lanes. The Street ends at Parkdale Avenue. On October 14, 2015, city council approved the renaming of the upper portion of Burlington Street from 165 meters east of Ottawa St to the QEW to Nikola Tesla Boulevard.

Nikola Tesla Boulevard is the official exit #90 from QEW. The signage along the QEW for Burlington Street was eventually replaced between October 22-23, 2016 to reflect the name change. After this change, the westbound lanes of Burlington street begin from the first exit on Nikola Tesla Boulevard, heading westbound.

Ahmed Javed (born 2 January 1956) is a retired Indian police officer and civil servant who was the 39th Commissioner of Police, Mumbai, India. He was succeeded by Dattatray Padsalgikar. He is former Indian Ambassador to Saudi Arabia. Javed is the second former Mumbai police commissioner to get a diplomatic posting after J. F. Ribeiro who served as Indian ambassador to Romania between 1989–1993.

Lau is a populated area, a socken (not to be confused with parish), on the Swedish island of Gotland. It comprises the same area as the administrative Lau District, established on 1 January 2016. Originally an island, it is now part of the main Gotland island due to the isostasy. It is mostly known for the good water from the spring Lau Käldu.

In July 2022, the Islamist militant group al-Shabaab launched an invasion from Somalia into Ethiopia's Somali Region. Following attacks on the Somali side of the border, the rebel militants initially attacked Ethiopia's Afder Zone on 21 July and occupied the town of Hulhul before being driven back by Somali Region paramilitary forces. On 25 July, the militants launched a second incursion at Ferfer which was also defeated. Further cross-border attacks continued in the following days, while Ethiopia launched counter-attacks in response. Clashes between the Somali rebels and security forces inside Ethiopia extended into early August, and at least one small al-Shabaab contingent succeeded in evading the Ethiopian force and reached its main target, the Bale Mountains.

The invasion was the largest attack by al-Shabaab in Ethiopian territory to date.

Chandler School is an independent, co-ed day school for students in kindergarten through grade eight located in Pasadena, California United States, founded in 1950. School enrollment is 450 students. The 4.5-acre (1.8 ha) campus overlooks the Pasadena Rose Bowl and the Arroyo Seco.

The men's horizontal bar competition at the 2016 Summer Olympics was held at the HSBC Arena on 6 and 16 August. There were 71 competitors from 34 nations. The event was won by Fabian Hambüchen of Germany, the nation's first victory in the horizontal bar since 1996 and third overall. Danell Leyva won the United States'third silver medal in four Games in the event; Nile Wilson's bronze was Great Britain's first-ever medal in the horizontal bar.

The medals were presented by Claudia Bokel IOC member, Germany and Naomi Valenzo, FIG Executive Committee Member.

Nagorik Natya Sampradaya is a Bangladeshi theatre company. Established in 1968, it was the first theatre group in Dhaka and started performing plays on stage in 1972. The chairperson of the group is the actor and director Aly Zaker and his wife, actress Sara Zaker, is the vice-chairperson. As of December 2018, Nagorik had performed 46 different productions.

Hope Hibbard (December 18, 1893- May 12, 1988) was an American biologist, cytologist, zoologist, and professor of zoology. Born in Altoona, Pennsylvania, she conducted research in the fields of histology and marine biology, utilizing organisms such as silkworms, limpets, earthworms, and frogs. Hibbard dedicated most of her life to education as a professor at multiple institutions, including Bryn Mawr College, Elmira College, and Oberlin College. She received accolades for her research and academic merits, such as the Sarah Berliner Research Fellowship and the Adelia A. Field Johnston Professor of Zoology. Hibbard is not only remembered for her intellectual endeavors, but for her support of women in the scientific sphere and her involvement in associations that forwarded female roles in science and research, such as the American Association of University Women (AAUW).

Oak Grove is a home rule-class city adjacent to the Fort Campbell army base in Christian County, Kentucky, in the United States. The population was 7,489 as of the 2010 U.S. Census. It is part of the Clarksville, Tennessee metropolitan area.

The Portuguese Faculdade de Ciências Sociais e Humanas (Faculty of Social and Human Sciences - FCSH) is an organic unit of the Universidade NOVA de Lisboa (NOVA). According to its statutes, “the Faculty of Social and Human Sciences of NOVA University of Lisbon is an institution dedicated to education, scientific research and cultural creation". The Faculty's own identity stems from the coexistence of social sciences with humanities, allowing an unusual interdisciplinarity in the Portuguese higher education panorama.

In the Night Room is a 2004 horror-thriller novel by American author Peter Straub, a sequel to his 2003 book Lost Boy, Lost Girl. The work was first published in hardback on October 26, 2004 through Random House and it won the 2004 Bram Stoker Award for Novel. Straub encountered some difficulties while writing In the Night Room and had written several different passages for the work before growing bored with each version before writing it using the same technique he used for its predecessor in which he "reached down inside the book and turned it inside out."

The long-billed curlew (Numenius americanus) is a large North American shorebird of the family Scolopacidae. This species was also called "sicklebird" and the "candlestick bird". The species breeds in central and western North America, migrating southward and coastward for the winter.
Academic ranks in the United States are the titles, relative importance and power of professors, researchers, and administrative personnel held in academia.

Crown Prince Hyoryeong (born 4 June 1149), personal name Wang Gi was a Goryeo Royal Prince as the first and oldest son of King Uijong and Queen Janggyeong who later deposed from his position alongside his father and his only son by Jeong Jung-bu and Yi Ui-bang in 1170.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer who made 124 appearances in the Football League for Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday and Oldham Athletic in the 1920s and 1930s. He played as a half-back – at wing half in the early part of his career, and at centre half later on – and could also play as a forward.

The Borscht Corporation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that creates short films and videos in and about the city of Miami, Florida. In addition to hosting a quasi-annual film festival that screens their work, Borscht Corp. provides financial and technical support to its commissioned filmmakers.

Since receiving a challenge grant from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation in 2010, Borscht Corp. has spearheaded productions by untested moviemakers and provided a creative platform for underrepresented (often Latin American) identities in film.

John James Hughes (1814 – 17 June 1889) was a Welsh engineer, businessman and founder of the city of Donetsk. The village was originally named Yuzovka (Hughesovka, Lang-ru) after Hughes, ("Yuz" being a Russian approximation of Hughes), formally becoming a town in May 1917 and later, in 1924, being renamed to Stalino (Lang-uk); in 1961, the name was changed to Donetsk.
Moritz Schuppert (1817 – May 2, 1887) was an American surgeon, anti-vaccinationist and early advocate of antisepsis.

Melchior Inchofer or Imhofer, in Hungarian: Inchofer Menyhért (c. 1584 – 28 September 1648) was an Austrian-Hungarian Jesuit. He played an important part in the trial of Galileo, by his arguments, later published in his Tractatus Syllepticus, that Galileo was an advocate of the Copernican system. His role in the Galileo affair is being reassessed in the light of fresh documentary evidence.

Petr Rada (born 21 August 1958) is a Czech football coach and former player. He represented Czechoslovakia internationally in the 1980s and managed the Czech Republic national football team between 2008 and 2009. He is well known for his arguments with fans or trainer colleagues and also for impulsive style of coaching.

Before being named national team manager in July 2008, Rada worked as an assistant for then national team coach Karel Brückner from 2006 to 2008, and also as an assistant to Jozef Chovanec from 1998 to 2001 when Chovanec was manager of the Czech national team. Rada has also been head manager of eight various Czech clubs: FK Teplice (on four separate occasions), FC Viktoria Plzeň, FK Jablonec, FC Slovan Liberec, SK Slavia Prague, FC Vysočina Jihlava, Příbram and Sparta Prague.

Capsule hotel (Lang-ja), also known in the Western world as a pod hotel, is a type of hotel developed in Japan that features many small bed-sized rooms known as capsules. Capsule hotels provide cheap, basic overnight accommodation for guests who do not require or who cannot afford larger, more expensive rooms offered by more conventional hotels.

The first capsule hotel in the world opened in 1979 and was the Capsule Inn Osaka, located in the Umeda district of Osaka, Japan and designed by Kisho Kurokawa. From there, it spread to other cities within Japan. Since then, the concept has further spread to various other territories, including Belgium, China, Hong Kong, Iceland, India, Indonesia, Poland and Canada.

Henry Arinze Anthony Ugboma (born 15 November 1966) is a Nigerian Professor of Maternal-fetal medicine and the present Chief Medical Director of the University of Port Harcourt Teaching Hospital, Rivers State.

Henry A. A. Ugboma is an alumnus of the University of Port Harcourt School of Medicine. He is a consultant, Feto-Maternal and infectious gynecologist and a Fellow of the International College of Surgeons – FICS. He had training in Leadership and Administration Processes in Rivers State. In 2016, he obtained a certificate of Professional Management from the Nigerian Institute of Management (charted). He further obtained training in Information and Communication Technology. In 2017, he became a member of the Administrative Staff College of Nigeria (ASCON). Consequently, in 2019, he was conferred a Fellow of the Institute of Health Service Administrators of Nigeria (IHSAN).

The Jackson Plan or Raffles Town Plan, an urban plan of 1822 titled "Plan of the Town of Singapore", is a proposed scheme for Singapore drawn up to maintain some order in the urban development of the fledgling but thriving colony founded just three years earlier. It was named after Lieutenant Philip Jackson, the colony's engineer and land surveyor tasked to oversee its physical development in accordance with the vision of Stamford Raffles for Singapore, hence it is also commonly called Raffles Town Plan. Raffles gave his instructions in November 1822, the plan was then drawn up in late 1822 or early 1823 and published in 1828. It is the earliest extant plan for the town of Singapore, but not an actual street map of Singapore as it existed in 1822 or 1827 since the plan is an idealised scheme of how Singapore may be organised that was not fully realised. Nevertheless, it served as a guide for the development of Singapore in its early days, and the effect of the general layout of the plan is still observable to this day.

The plan is currently on display in the Singapore History Gallery at the National Museum of Singapore.
